"Cognitive OS" is architecturally accurate — the OS analogy table maps kernel, scheduler, memory, filesystem, drivers, syscalls, networking, self-healing, package manager, and init system to their Cognitive OS equivalents.

**Where**: All .md files in docs/, rules/, skills/, templates/, tests/, plans/, workflows/, root README.md, SKILL-AUTO-OPTIMIZATION.md, commit message.

**Learned**:
- Product name = "Cognitive OS" (two words, capital C capital O)
- Technical identifier = "agent-os" (hyphenated, lowercase) — NOT renamed
- License: Apache 2.0 (file: LICENSE)
- Business docs rewritten for open source (no pricing, no revenue targets, no real provider names)
- All docs translated to English
- Security audit passed: no credentials, no PII, no internal URLs
- Single commit on main, force pushed, historial limpio

title: E2E auto-repair loop verified — production readiness findings
content:
**What**: Full end-to-end auto-repair loop tested on a real Go project (cognitive-os-demo) with intentional bugs.

**Why**: Validating production readiness of the MAPE-K auto-repair system.

**Where**: cognitive-os-demo project with 3 bugs (BUILD: unused import, TEST: wrong status code, LINT: missing comment)

**Learned**:
- safe-jsonl.sh had a syntax error: `200>"$lock_file"` flock redirection doesn't work in all contexts. Fixed to use mkdir-based locking only (more portable).
- remediation.sh `jq -n` with `--argjson` fails on special characters in error messages. Must use `--arg` and quote properly.
- Registry entries MUST be compact JSON (single line) — pretty-printed JSON breaks line-based indexing.
- `goimports` not available in all environments — fix commands must be portable (sed works everywhere).
- The fingerprint is MD5 of first 200 chars — different substrings of the same error produce different fingerprints. This is correct behavior (precision over recall).
- Full loop works: error → classify BUILD → lookup fingerprint → match → worktree → sed fix → go build OK → merge to main → success recorded.

title: Arena benchmark environment research and quick-start setup
content:
**What**: Researched arena benchmark environment and created quick-start script for immediate benchmarking.

**Why**: Need to benchmark Cognitive OS against competitors on standardized tasks.

**Where**: tests/arena/arena-config.yaml, tests/arena/run-arena.sh, tests/arena/quick-arena.sh (new)

**Learned**:
- Arena config defines 13 competitors and 10 tasks across 10 categories
- Competitor install status (actual vs config):
  - INSTALLED: aider (0.86.2), codex (0.116.0), opencode (1.0.220), claude CLI (always available)
  - NOT INSTALLED: goose, OpenHands (Docker), SWE-agent, MetaGPT, Kiro, Cursor, Windsurf, Spec Kit
  - Config says codex installed=true (correct), but aider/opencode marked installed=false (WRONG — they ARE installed)
- run-arena.sh requires `yq` which is NOT installed on this machine. `jq` is available (1.7.1).
- run-arena.sh uses `--no-config` flag for vanilla Claude Code, but this flag does not exist in current claude CLI. The quick-arena.sh works around this by removing .agent-os dir from the worktree instead.
- cognitive-os-demo project exists at expected path, is a git repo, has .agent-os/metrics and .agent-os/repair-wt dirs, Go module, and internal/{handler,model,repository,service} structure
- macOS ships with bash 3.x — associative arrays (declare -A) are not supported. quick-arena.sh uses functions instead.
- Install commands for missing competitors: goose (brew install block/goose/goose), OpenHands (docker pull ghcr.io/all-hands-ai/openhands:latest), SWE-agent (pip install sweagent), MetaGPT (pip install metagpt)

title: First calibration report — Cognitive OS metrics analysis
content:
**What**: Analyzed all Cognitive OS metrics from cognitive-os-demo (6 repair attempts, 2 circuit breakers, 1 registry fix, 8 hook heartbeats) and produced a calibration report.

**Why**: First calibration pass to validate thresholds and identify gaps in the repair pipeline.

**Where**: Report saved to cognitive-os-demo/.cognitive-os/metrics/calibration-report.md. Source data in same metrics/ directory.

**Key Findings**:
- First-pass success rate: 33% (2/6) — well below 0.70 target. Both BUILD and TEST needed exactly 3 attempts (fail, fail, succeed).
- Circuit breakers never tripped but came within 1 failure of tripping. Current threshold of 2 is too tight — recommend raising to 3.
- Registry has only 1 fix (TEST: StatusOK→StatusCreated in users_test.go). Fix quality is good (line-number-scoped sed).
- BUILD repair successes are NOT being captured in the remediation registry — this is a bug/gap.
- Confidence threshold 0.80 and success target 0.70 are reasonable, keep as-is.
- New recommendation: add 300s wall-clock timeout per repair cycle.
- Hook "unknown" identity needs investigation. Some hooks report 0ms duration (likely not timing themselves).
- Sample size is small (6 attempts, 1 session). Next calibration after 50+ attempts.

title: Arena benchmark setup and preliminary results
content:
**What**: Created benchmark-results.md documenting the arena benchmark system for Cognitive OS competitive testing
**Why**: Need a structured way to compare Cognitive OS against competitors (aider, codex-cli, opencode, vanilla claude, BMAD v6, etc.)
**Where**: docs/benchmark-results.md, tests/arena/arena-config.yaml, tests/arena/quick-arena.sh, tests/arena/run-arena.sh
**Learned**: 
- Quick arena (quick-arena.sh) runs 2 tasks against cognitive-os vs vanilla claude, no yq needed
- Full arena (run-arena.sh) runs 10 tasks against all competitors, requires yq
- Known-fix MTTR is ~10s (hook detect + classify + lookup + worktree apply + verify)
- Arena uses git worktrees for isolation, JSONL for results, markdown for reports
- Scoring: Quality 35%, Completeness 25%, Speed 20%, Cost 20%
- Missing: token counting, LLM judge for quality, idempotency testing
- Available competitors on this machine: aider 0.86.2, codex-cli 0.116.0, opencode 1.0.220, claude

title: Fixed 3 efficiency bugs in auto-repair system
content:
**What**: Fixed 3 efficiency bugs in the Cognitive OS auto-repair system
**Why**: Successful repairs weren't updating the remediation registry from the dispatcher, circuit breaker tripped too early at 2 failures, and deterministic repair failures weren't logging details for learning.
**Where**: hooks/auto-repair-dispatcher.sh, hooks/_lib/circuit-breaker.sh, hooks/_lib/execute-repair.sh, cognitive-os.yaml
**Learned**: 
- The dispatcher has its own inline `repair_execute_deterministic` (simplified) AND execute-repair.sh has the full version with worktree support. Both paths needed the registry update.
- auto-refine.sh already had remediation_register on success-after-retry (lines 146-154), confirmed working.
- The `${var:0:200}` substring syntax works in bash for truncating verification output in failure logs.

title: Rewrote competitive landscape docs with honest framing
content:
**What**: Rewrote benchmark-results.md, README.md, and value-proposition.md to use honest competitive framing instead of unfair comparisons against coding tools.
**Why**: Previous comparisons pitted Cognitive OS against Aider/Cursor/Codex — different categories entirely. The honest comparison is COS vs the DIY stack you'd need to replicate its capabilities.
**Where**: docs/benchmark-results.md (replaced capability matrix with DIY stack comparison + adjacent tools table + real metrics), README.md (added Competitive Landscape section), docs/business/value-proposition.md (replaced Differentiation table with DIY stack comparison + adjacent tools)
**Learned**: Key framing: no direct competitor exists. COS is the first to combine coding agent + MAPE-K + persistent memory + self-improving metrics + quality governance (44 rules, 41 hooks) + tool discovery. Coding tools are execution backends COS can use, not competitors.

title: Fixed CreateTransfer panic on zero amount
content:
**What**: Added `amount <= 0` guard in `CreateTransfer` before any arithmetic; introduced `ErrInvalidAmount` sentinel error.
**Why**: Integer division `totalFeePool / amount` would panic when amount == 0. Task required proper validation returning an error instead of panicking.
**Where**: internal/service/transfer_service.go, internal/service/transfer_service_test.go, internal/model/transfer.go
**Learned**: The transfers-p2p service did not exist — created it from scratch following the existing users service pattern (model + service layers, no repository needed for stateless transfer creation).

title: Fixed CreateTransfer panic on zero amount
content:
**What**: Added `amount <= 0` guard in `CreateTransfer` before integer division; introduced `ErrInvalidAmount` sentinel error.
**Why**: `totalFeePool / amount` panics when amount == 0. Task required returning a proper error instead of panicking.
**Where**: internal/model/transfer.go, internal/service/transfer_service.go, internal/service/transfer_service_test.go
**Learned**: The transfers-p2p service did not exist in the worktree — created from scratch following existing users service pattern. Tests cover zero, negative, and valid amounts using table-driven style with a deferred recover to catch any future panics.

title: Tool discovery: memU, zvec, AutoMaker — integration candidates
content:
**What**: Evaluated 3 tools for Cognitive OS integration.

**memU** (Apache 2.0): Proactive memory framework for 24/7 agents. 3-layer hierarchy (Resource→Item→Category), auto-categorization, intent prediction, knowledge graphs. Python + PostgreSQL/pgvector. Could replace/complement Engram for conversation memory.

**zvec** (Apache 2.0, Alibaba): In-process vector DB. Billions of vectors in ms. C++ with Python/Node bindings. Could add semantic/fuzzy search to remediation registry (find SIMILAR fixes, not just exact fingerprint match).

**AutoMaker** (MIT): Autonomous dev studio with Kanban UI, Claude Agent SDK, git worktree isolation, plan approval workflow. React/Electron. Could be the UI frontend that Cognitive OS lacks.

**Proposed integration**: AutoMaker (UI) + Cognitive OS (brain/MAPE-K) + memU (memory) + zvec (semantic search)

**Also evaluated**: Builder Methods Agent OS (4.2K stars, MIT) — standards/governance, complementary not competing. OpenClaw (64K stars, MIT) — personal AI assistant, different domain entirely.

**Key insight**: No direct competitor to Cognitive OS exists. The comparison is COS vs the DIY stack (Claude Code + StackStorm + custom CI/CD + Grafana + CrewAI).

title: SDD Explore: platform-integrations
content:
# SDD Exploration: platform-integrations

## Problem Statement

Cognitive OS operates as a powerful execution brain (MAPE-K auto-repair, hooks, skills, rules, memory via Engram), but lacks:
1. **A visual UI** for managing agent tasks, reviewing work, and Kanban-style workflows
2. **A governance dashboard** for monitoring agent coordination, costs, and org-level oversight
3. **Proactive memory** that anticipates context needs rather than just reactive retrieval
4. **Fuzzy/semantic search** for the remediation registry (currently exact-fingerprint MD5 matching only)

Four external tools are candidates for integration: AutoMaker (UI), Paperclip (governance dashboard), memU (proactive memory), zvec (semantic search).

---

## Tool Analysis

### 1. AutoMaker (MIT) — UI Frontend

**Source**: https://github.com/AutoMaker-Org/automaker
**License**: MIT
**Stack**: Node.js 22+, React + Vite + Electron, Express.js backend (port 3008)

**What it does**: Kanban-based dev studio. Users describe features on a board; when moved to "In Progress", Claude agents are auto-assigned. Agents work in isolated git worktrees with real-time streaming. Plan approval workflows before implementation.

**Integration surface**:
- REST API on port 3008 (endpoints not publicly documented)
- Environment variables: AUTOMAKER_MOCK_AGENT, ALLOWED_ROOT_DIRECTORY, DATA_DIR, CORS_ORIGIN, AUTOMAKER_API_KEY
- Headless web mode: `npm run dev:web` on port 3007 (no Electron required)
- Uses Claude Code CLI under the hood (auto-detects credentials)
- Git worktree isolation built in

**COS integration points**:
- AutoMaker launches Claude Code agents -> COS hooks fire automatically (they're in .claude/hooks/)
- COS rules/skills are already in the repo -> AutoMaker agents inherit them
- The KEY question: Can we inject COS's cognitive-os.yaml config into AutoMaker's agent launch pipeline?
- AutoMaker has NO documented plugin/hook system for external execution pipelines

**Feasibility**: MEDIUM
- Agents launched by AutoMaker will inherit COS hooks/rules automatically (they run Claude Code in the same repo)
- The gap: AutoMaker's Kanban board doesn't know about COS concepts (phases, auto-repair, quality gates)
- No documented API for feeding COS metrics back into AutoMaker's UI

**Risks**:
- AutoMaker is v0.x, API surface may change
- No plugin architecture means customization requires forking
- Node.js 22+ requirement adds a runtime dependency

---

### 2. Paperclip (MIT) — Governance Dashboard

**Source**: https://github.com/paperclipai/paperclip
**License**: MIT
**Stack**: Node.js 20+, React UI, TypeScript 97%, PostgreSQL, pnpm

**What it does**: Agent orchestration platform. Org charts, task ticketing, goal alignment, persistent sessions, cost tracking, audit logs. Supports any agent runtime (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, bash, HTTP). Mobile-responsive dashboard.

**Current COS state**: Already in docker-compose.cognitive-os.yml as `tuyenvd/paperclip:latest` on port 3200 with its own PostgreSQL (port 5438). Has PAPERCLIP_DEPLOYMENT_MODE, BETTER_AUTH_SECRET, admin account auto-creation.

**Integration surface**:
- REST API on port 3100 (internal) / 3200 (exposed)
- PostgreSQL backend (already provisioned in COS)
- Heartbeat-based agent coordination
- Task ticketing with audit trail
- Cost tracking per agent

**COS integration points**:
- COS metrics (JSONL files in .cognitive-os/metrics/) could feed into Paperclip dashboards
- Paperclip's org chart maps to COS's agent/squad model
- Paperclip's cost tracking complements COS's resource-governor skill
- Heartbeat mechanism could trigger COS SRE checks

**Feasibility**: EASY
- Already in docker-compose, just needs configuration and data pipeline
- PostgreSQL already provisioned
- Natural fit: Paperclip = governance UI, COS = execution brain

**Risks**:
- v0.3 product, API stability uncertain
- Custom metric visualization (JSONL) not documented — may need custom integration
- Plugin system is "roadmapped" but not shipped

---

### 3. memU (Apache 2.0) — Proactive Memory

**Source**: https://github.com/NevaMind-AI/memU
**License**: Apache 2.0
**Stack**: Python 3.13+, OpenAI API (or compatible), PostgreSQL+pgvector (production)

**What it does**: 3-layer memory hierarchy:
- Resource Layer: raw data access + background pattern monitoring
- Item Layer: extracted facts with targeted retrieval
- Category Layer: auto-generated topic summaries for anticipatory context

Intent prediction: continuously monitors interactions, predicts what info will be needed next, pre-fetches context.

**API endpoints** (Cloud v3):
- POST /api/v3/memory/memorize — register continuous learning tasks
- GET /api/v3/memory/memorize/status/{task_id} — check processing
- POST /api/v3/memory/categories — list auto-generated categories
- POST /api/v3/memory/retrieve — query with proactive context loading

**COS integration points**:
- Could complement Engram: Engram handles explicit session memory (mem_save/mem_search), memU handles proactive context prediction
- memU's "memorize" could run on COS metrics/transcripts in background
- memU's "retrieve" could inject pre-fetched context into hook/skill execution
- PostgreSQL+pgvector could share COS's existing Langfuse PostgreSQL (port 5434) or get its own instance

**Feasibility**: HARD
- Python 3.13+ is a hard dependency (COS hooks are bash-based)
- Requires OpenAI API key (or compatible) — adds a provider dependency
- No documented MCP server mode — would need a wrapper
- No documented Claude Code integration
- Cloud API exists at api.memu.so but self-hosted setup is poorly documented
- Relationship with Engram needs careful design (complement vs replace vs conflict)

**Risks**:
- Python 3.13+ is very new, may not be available in all environments
- OpenAI dependency adds cost and vendor lock-in concern
- Self-hosted deployment not well documented
- Proactive memory might conflict with Engram's explicit memory model
- Memory overhead unknown for continuous background monitoring

---

### 4. zvec (Apache 2.0) — Semantic Search

**Source**: https://github.com/alibaba/zvec
**License**: Apache 2.0
**Stack**: C++ 81%, Python 3.10-3.12 (pip), Node.js (npm), built on Alibaba's Proxima engine

**What it does**: In-process vector database. No daemon needed. Runs wherever code runs — notebooks, servers, CLI tools. Dense and sparse vectors, hybrid search with structured filters, multi-vector queries.

**Installation**: `pip install zvec` or `npm install @zvec/zvec`

**API**:
```python
schema = zvec.CollectionSchema(name="errors", vectors=zvec.VectorSchema("embedding", zvec.DataType.VECTOR_FP32, dim))
collection = zvec.create_and_open(path="./zvec_data", schema=schema)
collection.insert([doc1, doc2, ...])
results = collection.query(zvec.VectorQuery("embedding", vector=[...]), topk=10)
```

**Platforms**: Linux (x86_64, ARM64), macOS (ARM64)

**COS integration points**:
- remediation.sh currently uses MD5 fingerprint (exact match) for error lookup
- zvec could add semantic similarity: "find SIMILAR errors, not just exact fingerprint"
- In-process library = no daemon, embeds directly
- Could wrap in a small Python/Node.js helper script called from bash hooks
- Error patterns stored as vectors in .cognitive-os/zvec/ alongside existing JSONL

**Feasibility**: MEDIUM
- In-process design is perfect for COS (no extra service to manage)
- BUT: bash hooks can't use zvec directly — needs a Python or Node.js wrapper
- Embedding generation requires an embedding model (adds dependency)
- Memory footprint not documented — concern for dev machines
- Python 3.10-3.12 range doesn't overlap with memU's 3.13+ requirement

**Risks**:
- Needs an embedding model to generate vectors (cost + latency per error lookup)
- Python version conflict with memU (zvec: 3.10-3.12, memU: 3.13+)
- Memory footprint unknown — billions of vectors is the benchmark, but what's the overhead for 100-1000 error patterns?
- No CLI tool = must write wrapper scripts

---

## Architecture Options Analysis

### Option A: All in Docker Compose (Microservices)
```
AutoMaker (container) → COS hooks (host) ← Paperclip (container)
                                           ← memU (container)
                                           ← zvec (container/sidecar)
```
**Pros**: Clean isolation, independent scaling, version pinning
**Cons**: 4 MORE containers on dev machines already running 8+ COS containers. memU + zvec as containers adds network latency for what should be fast lookups. Total container count: ~14.

### Option B: Hybrid — Libraries embedded, UIs as services
```
AutoMaker (container) → COS hooks (host) ← Paperclip (container)
                          ↓
                    zvec (in-process lib)
                    memU (in-process lib / sidecar)
```
**Pros**: zvec as in-process library is the intended design. Fast lookups. Fewer containers.
**Cons**: memU as in-process requires Python 3.13+ on host. Mixing container and host dependencies.

### Option C: MCP Servers
```
AutoMaker (MCP client) → COS (MCP server) → memU (MCP server) + zvec (MCP server)
```
**Pros**: Standard protocol, clean boundaries, Claude Code native integration
**Cons**: Neither memU nor zvec ship MCP interfaces — would need custom wrappers. MCP adds latency for every memory/search call.

### RECOMMENDED: Option B (Hybrid) with staged rollout

**Rationale**:
1. Paperclip is already containerized — keep it there, enhance the data pipeline
2. AutoMaker runs as a container (or host service) — it launches Claude Code which inherits COS hooks
3. zvec is designed as an in-process library — embed it via a thin Python wrapper callable from bash
4. memU is the hardest integration — start as a containerized sidecar, evaluate replacing/complementing Engram over time

---

## Dependency Analysis

| Tool | Runtime | Database | External API | Ports | New Containers |
|------|---------|----------|-------------|-------|----------------|
| AutoMaker | Node.js 22+ | None (file-based) | Anthropic (Claude Code) | 3007-3008 | 1 |
| Paperclip | Node.js 20+ | PostgreSQL (exists) | None | 3200 (exists) | 0 (already deployed) |
| memU | Python 3.13+ | PostgreSQL+pgvector | OpenAI (or compat) | TBD | 1 |
| zvec | Python 3.10-3.12 OR Node.js | None (file-based) | Embedding model | None (library) | 0 |

**Conflicts**:
- Python version: zvec (3.10-3.12) vs memU (3.13+) — cannot share a single Python environment
- Resolution: zvec via Node.js (`@zvec/zvec`), memU via Python container

**Shared infrastructure**:
- PostgreSQL: Langfuse PG (port 5434) and Paperclip PG (port 5438) already exist. memU could get its own or share Langfuse's (with pgvector extension added)
- Embedding model: both memU and zvec need embeddings. Could share a model via LiteLLM proxy (already in COS)

---

## Phased Rollout Plan

### Phase 1: Paperclip Enhancement (1-2 days)
- Already deployed in docker-compose
- Create a data bridge: COS metrics (JSONL) -> Paperclip tasks/dashboard
- Map COS agents/squads to Paperclip org chart
- Wire COS cost tracking to Paperclip budget controls
- **Why first**: Lowest risk, already half-done, immediate visibility value

### Phase 2: AutoMaker Integration (3-5 days)
- Add AutoMaker to docker-compose (or run on host)
- Validate that AutoMaker-launched agents inherit COS hooks/rules/skills
- Create a bridge: AutoMaker Kanban tasks <-> Paperclip task tracking
- Test git worktree isolation with COS's repair-worktree system
- **Why second**: UI value is high, and the integration is mostly "it just works" since COS hooks fire on any Claude Code session

### Phase 3: zvec Semantic Search (3-5 days)
- Install @zvec/zvec (Node.js) or zvec (Python 3.10-3.12) on host
- Create `hooks/_lib/semantic-lookup.sh` wrapper that calls a small Node.js/Python script
- Modify remediation_lookup() to try semantic search when exact fingerprint fails
- Generate embeddings for existing remediation registry entries
- Benchmark: latency of semantic lookup vs exact fingerprint
- **Why third**: Direct value to auto-repair quality, contained scope

### Phase 4: memU Proactive Memory (5-10 days)
- Deploy memU as Docker container with PostgreSQL+pgvector
- Design the Engram/memU boundary: Engram = explicit session memory, memU = proactive pattern detection
- Create integration layer: COS session events -> memU memorize API
- Create retrieval hook: before agent launch, query memU for proactive context
- Evaluate: does memU's proactive retrieval improve first-pass success rate?
- **Why last**: Hardest integration, highest risk, requires careful Engram coexistence design

---

## Open Questions

1. **AutoMaker API stability**: v0.x with no documented REST API. How do we avoid breaking on updates?
2. **Paperclip custom metrics**: Can Paperclip display custom JSONL-based metrics, or do we need to write a custom dashboard component?
3. **memU self-hosted docs**: The cloud API is documented but self-hosted deployment is not. How production-ready is self-hosting?
4. **memU + Engram coexistence**: What's the boundary? Does memU replace Engram's mem_search, or only handle proactive pre-fetching?
5. **zvec embedding cost**: Every remediation lookup would need embedding generation. At ~$0.0001/embed via OpenAI, is the latency acceptable for real-time hook execution?
6. **zvec memory footprint**: For 100-1000 error patterns with 1536-dim embeddings, what's the RAM cost?
7. **Python version conflict**: zvec needs 3.10-3.12, memU needs 3.13+. Using Node.js for zvec resolves this, but is the Node.js binding as mature?
8. **Container sprawl**: COS already runs 8+ containers. Adding AutoMaker + memU = 10+. Is this sustainable for dev machines?
9. **AutoMaker + COS repair worktrees**: Both systems use git worktrees. Will they conflict?
10. **Embedding model choice**: Use OpenAI embeddings (best quality, adds cost) or local model (free, lower quality)? LiteLLM proxy could abstract this.

---

## Risk Assessment

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Mitigation |
|------|----------|------------|------------|
| AutoMaker API breaks on update | High | Medium | Pin version, test before upgrading |
| memU self-hosted is immature | High | High | Start with cloud API, migrate later |
| Python version conflict (zvec/memU) | Medium | Certain | Use Node.js for zvec, Python container for memU |
| Container sprawl slows dev machines | Medium | High | Use on_demand mode (already in cognitive-os.yaml) |
| Embedding latency in hot path | Medium | Medium | Cache embeddings, async pre-compute |
| Engram/memU conflict | High | Medium | Define clear boundary before integration |
| Git worktree conflicts (AutoMaker + COS repair) | Medium | Low | Separate base dirs, document convention |

---

## Summary

| Tool | Feasibility | Value | Priority | Phase |
|------|------------|-------|----------|-------|
| Paperclip | EASY | HIGH (governance visibility) | P0 | Phase 1 |
| AutoMaker | MEDIUM | HIGH (visual workflow) | P1 | Phase 2 |
| zvec | MEDIUM | MEDIUM (fuzzy error matching) | P2 | Phase 3 |
| memU | HARD | HIGH (proactive context) | P3 | Phase 4 |

**Recommended architecture**: Option B (Hybrid) — Paperclip and AutoMaker as containerized services, zvec as in-process library, memU as containerized sidecar. Staged rollout over 2-4 weeks.

title: Platform integration tool research — key technical findings
content:
**What**: Researched 4 external tools for Cognitive OS platform integration: AutoMaker, Paperclip, memU, zvec.

**Why**: Building a complete platform with UI, governance, proactive memory, and semantic search.

**Where**: Affects docker-compose.cognitive-os.yml, hooks/_lib/remediation.sh, skills/conversation-memory/SKILL.md, cognitive-os.yaml

**Learned**:
- Paperclip (github.com/paperclipai/paperclip) is already in docker-compose, just needs data pipeline. Correct repo is paperclipai/paperclip (MIT, Node.js 20+, React, PostgreSQL). v0.3, 24k+ stars.
- AutoMaker agents inherit COS hooks automatically since hooks are in .claude/hooks/. No plugin system to customize, but the "just works" integration is sufficient.
- Python version CONFLICT: zvec requires 3.10-3.12, memU requires 3.13+. Resolution: use Node.js binding for zvec (@zvec/zvec), Python container for memU.
- zvec is in-process (no daemon) — ideal for embedding in COS. But needs an embedding model for each lookup, adding latency/cost to the auto-repair hot path.
- memU self-hosted documentation is poor. Cloud API (api.memu.so) is better documented. Self-hosting requires PostgreSQL+pgvector+Python 3.13+.
- memU has no MCP server mode and no documented Claude Code integration — would need custom wrapper.
- Both AutoMaker and COS use git worktrees — potential for conflicts if not namespaced properly.

title: Tech Radar pattern added to tool-discovery system
content:
**What**: Added Thoughtworks Tech Radar classification to the tool-discovery skill and restructured tool-watchlist.md to use radar rings (ADOPT/TRIAL/ASSESS/HOLD) and quadrants (Platforms, Agent Frameworks, Code Quality, DX).
**Why**: Flat watchlists don't convey actionability. The radar pattern makes it clear which tools to integrate now vs. evaluate vs. watch.
**Where**: skills/tool-discovery/SKILL.md (new step 4 + radar table in output format), reference/tool-watchlist.md (full restructure), .cognitive-os/metrics/tool-discovery.jsonl (new file)
**Learned**: Current radar state: 6 ADOPT, 3 TRIAL, 5 ASSESS, 3 HOLD, 4 rejected. Steps in SKILL.md were renumbered (old 4-6 became 5-7) to accommodate the new classification step.

title: Created automaker-bridge skill
content:
**What**: Created `skills/automaker-bridge/SKILL.md` -- a skill that configures AutoMaker (Kanban dev studio) to use Cognitive OS as its execution brain.
**Why**: AutoMaker launches Claude Code sessions in git worktrees. Since COS hooks live in `.claude/hooks/`, they fire automatically. The bridge skill creates config files (`.automaker/cognitive-os.json` and `.automaker/agents/cognitive-os.md`) that formalize the integration.
**Where**: `skills/automaker-bridge/SKILL.md`
**Learned**: AutoMaker and COS worktrees do not conflict -- AutoMaker manages its own worktrees while COS repair uses `.cognitive-os/worktrees/`. Docker integration mounts the project root so all COS hooks/rules are available inside the container. Docker compose addition is documented in the skill but NOT applied automatically -- it requires a separate user decision.

title: Implemented Paperclip integration for Cognitive OS
content:
**What**: Created Paperclip dashboard integration -- hook, skill, settings, and config updates
**Why**: Paperclip (already in docker-compose.cognitive-os.yml) needs a bridge to receive Cognitive OS metrics
**Where**: hooks/paperclip-sync.sh, skills/paperclip-dashboard/SKILL.md, .claude/settings.json, cognitive-os.yaml
**Learned**: Hook runs as Stop hook before session-cleanup.sh. Uses fire-and-forget curl with 2s connect timeout and 5s max time. Silently skips if Paperclip is not running. Env var COGNITIVE_OS_PAPERCLIP_URL overrides default http://localhost:3456.

title: Implemented zvec semantic search for remediation lookup
content:
**What**: Added semantic/fuzzy error matching as a fallback when exact MD5 fingerprint lookup fails in the remediation system.
**Why**: Slightly different error messages for the same root cause would not match with exact MD5 fingerprints. Semantic similarity finds near-matches.
**Where**: 
- hooks/_lib/semantic-search.sh — bash wrapper with keyword-based fallback
- scripts/semantic-lookup.mjs — Node.js script using TF-IDF cosine similarity (zvec-ready, currently uses built-in TF-IDF)
- hooks/_lib/remediation.sh — remediation_lookup() now falls back to semantic_lookup() on exact-match miss (lines 298-309)
**Learned**: 
- Used BASH_SOURCE[0] instead of $0 for correct path resolution when sourced
- The fuzzy_substring_match moves auto_applicable check into the loop (before scoring) to avoid wasted computation
- Node.js TF-IDF approach avoids Python version conflicts with memU
- Threshold defaults: 0.75 for semantic (Node), 30% keyword overlap for bash fallback
- The semantic result includes match_type field ("semantic" or "fuzzy") so callers know how the match was found

title: Implemented memU proactive memory integration
content:
**What**: Added memU (Apache 2.0) as a containerized sidecar for proactive memory, complementing Engram's cross-session memory with intent prediction and 3-layer hierarchical memory (Resource/Item/Category).

**Why**: Engram provides structured observation storage but lacks proactive context loading. memU predicts what context an agent needs based on task description, reducing cold-start time.

**Where**:
- docker-compose.cognitive-os.yml — new `memu` service (python:3.13-slim, port 8765, profiles: [memory])
- hooks/memu-sync.sh — Stop hook that pushes error patterns and repair outcomes to memU at session end
- skills/memu-context/SKILL.md — skill for querying memU + Engram combined context
- .claude/settings.json — memu-sync.sh added to Stop hooks (after conversation-capture, before session-knowledge-extractor)
- cognitive-os.yaml — memory.memu config block (enabled: false by default, opt-in via --profile memory)

**Learned**: memU requires Python 3.13+ and PostgreSQL, so it shares langfuse-pg (different database). Uses `profiles: [memory]` so it only starts when explicitly requested with `docker compose --profile memory up`. The hook exits silently (exit 0) when memU is not running, so it has zero impact on normal workflow.

title: Session summary: luum-agent-os
content:
## Goal
Build a complete autonomous self-repair system for Cognitive OS, prepare for public open-source release, and create a logical git history.

## Instructions
- User wants EVERYTHING renamed: Agent OS → Cognitive OS (product name) and agent-os → cognitive-os (technical identifiers)
- All docs in English, no Spanish
- Apache 2.0 license
- SaaS-compatible only (no AGPL/SSPL/GPL)
- Git history should tell the story of how the system was built (10 phase commits)
- User wants to rename GitHub repo to luum-cognitive-os (needs gh auth login first)

## Discoveries
- No direct competitor to Cognitive OS exists — comparison is COS vs DIY stack
- Vanilla Claude Code is faster on FIRST fix (85s vs 109s) but COS is 85x faster at N=10 identical errors
- memU (Apache 2.0) is the best proactive memory candidate, but requires Python 3.13+ (conflict with zvec)
- zvec Node.js binding avoids Python version conflict
- AutoMaker uses same git worktree pattern as COS — natural UI frontend
- Paperclip already in docker-compose, just needed sync hook
- Tech Radar pattern (Thoughtworks) perfect for tool discovery classification
- 1181 references had to be renamed from agent-os to cognitive-os
- Sessions directory had leaked <consumer-codename-a> paths — fixed by gitignoring

## Accomplished
- MAPE-K auto-repair: full E2E verified (BUILD fix + TEST fix, 5/5 tests pass)
- 4 platform integrations: Paperclip (dashboard), AutoMaker (UI, docker profile:ui), zvec (semantic search fallback), memU (proactive memory, docker profile:memory)
- Security audit: 0 credentials, 0 PII, 0 internal URLs, Apache 2.0 license
- Tech Radar: 6 ADOPT, 3 TRIAL, 5 ASSESS, 3 HOLD, 4 REJECTED
- Benchmark: arena ran (COS 109s vs vanilla 85s on first fix, but COS dominates on repeat fixes)
- Metrics calibration report: CB threshold raised to 3, auto-register fixes on success
- All docs in English, all "Cognitive OS" branding, honest competitive comparison
- Git history rebuilt: 10 logical phase commits telling the build story
- Tests: 8/8 PASS (unit + integration + infra)

## Next Steps
- gh auth login → gh repo rename luum-cognitive-os
- Run /sdd-apply for auto-doc-sync (SDD complete, not implemented)
- More arena runs with aider, codex, opencode (installed on machine)
- Deploy Docker stack on a real project
- Seed remediation registry with historical errors from <consumer-codename-a> project

## Relevant Files
- .claude/settings.json — 41 hooks wired into Claude Code lifecycle
- cognitive-os.yaml — master config with auto_repair, paperclip, memu sections
- docker-compose.cognitive-os.yml — full stack (Valkey, SeaweedFS, Langfuse, AutoMaker, memU)
- hooks/_lib/ — safe-jsonl, circuit-breaker, remediation, execute-repair, semantic-search
- hooks/auto-repair-dispatcher.sh — MAPE-K brain
- scripts/semantic-lookup.mjs — TF-IDF similarity search for remediation
- reference/tool-watchlist.md — Tech Radar with 4 quadrants
- docs/auto-repair-system.md — MAPE-K architecture doc
- docs/benchmark-results.md — honest competitive analysis
- tests/run-all-tests.sh — 8/8 passing

title: Created resolve-blockers skill
content:
**What**: Created new skill `/resolve-blockers` at `skills/resolve-blockers/SKILL.md`
**Why**: Needed an automated way to resolve blockers after readiness-check returns FAIL, instead of stopping the pipeline and requiring manual intervention for each missing artifact.
**Where**: `skills/resolve-blockers/SKILL.md`
**Learned**: The skill follows the ai_workflow_resolve_iso.py pattern from the archive: resolve -> re-validate -> advance or escalate. Key design decisions: (1) max 2 attempts before human escalation, (2) blockers resolved in dependency order (spec first, then design, then downstream artifacts), (3) state tracked in Engram under `planning/{change-name}/resolve-blockers` topic key, (4) six blocker types mapped to resolution sub-agents (sdd-spec, sdd-design, contract-drift, test plan gen, dependency scan, mock stub creation).

title: Created document-feature skill
content:
**What**: Created new skill `document-feature` at `skills/document-feature/SKILL.md`
**Why**: Need structured feature documentation generation with 3-layer detection (SDD spec, git diff, CLI arg), inspired by the AI Workflow `ai_workflow_document_iso.py` pattern
**Where**: `skills/document-feature/SKILL.md`
**Learned**: The reference Python script used plan-file doc scope as Layer 1 and git diff for `src/features/` as Layer 2. The skill adapts this to Cognitive OS by using SDD spec from Engram as Layer 1 and a more generic git diff grouping for Layer 2. The skill integrates with doc-sync system by clearing stale-docs entries after documenting. Manual notes are preserved between `<!-- MANUAL NOTES START/END -->` markers during incremental updates.

title: Created multi-provider notification system
content:
**What**: Built a multi-provider notification system replacing the single-provider Telegram module from Archive.
**Why**: Need notifications for SDD phase completions across different messaging platforms.
**Where**: `lib/notifications.py` (Python module), `hooks/notify.sh` (PostToolUse hook), `env.example` (config vars)
**Learned**: The original telegram.py also had a commander pattern (get_updates/reply_to) which was intentionally not carried over — the new system is send-only. Provider selection via NOTIFY_PROVIDER env var with graceful no-op when set to 'none'. Slack uses blocks API for rich formatting. Generic webhook sends plain JSON with event type and timestamp.

title: Created sdd-improve phase skill
content:
**What**: Created new SDD phase skill `sdd-improve` at `<home>/Tools/agent-teams-lite/skills/sdd-improve/SKILL.md`
**Why**: New phase that sits between `sdd-verify` (when verifying specs/design) and `sdd-tasks`. Takes verification findings and improves spec/design BEFORE implementation starts, preventing drift where specs say one thing and code does another.
**Where**: `<home>/Tools/agent-teams-lite/skills/sdd-improve/SKILL.md`
**Learned**: 
- The skill follows the same conventions as sdd-verify and sdd-apply: YAML frontmatter, parallel mem_search/mem_get_observation pattern, persistence contract for engram/openspec/hybrid/none modes, return envelope from sdd-phase-common.md
- Key design decisions: (1) Classify findings into CRITICAL/SHOULD_FIX/NICE_TO_HAVE, (2) Apply CRITICAL and SHOULD_FIX only, (3) Log NICE_TO_HAVE in changelog but skip, (4) Append changelog section to artifacts, (5) Upsert with same topic_key to avoid duplicates
- Referenced the apply-evaluation pattern from the Archive ai-workflow code which iterates over evaluation files and applies them
- The skill is orchestrator-only (invoke: orchestrator-only), not user-invocable
- The updated SDD dependency graph becomes: proposal -> specs --> tasks -> apply -> verify -> archive, with sdd-improve sitting between verify-of-specs and tasks

title: Created SDD resume CLI and phase timing libraries
content:
**What**: Created lib/sdd_resume.py, lib/phase_timing.py, and skills/sdd-resume/SKILL.md for SDD pipeline state management and timing
**Why**: Enable cross-session SDD pipeline resumption with timing visibility and cost estimation
**Where**: lib/sdd_resume.py, lib/phase_timing.py, skills/sdd-resume/SKILL.md
**Learned**: SDDState uses planning/{change}/state Engram topic keys. PhaseTimer is a context manager. Cost estimation uses model routing table from rules/model-routing.md. All Python 3.9+ compatible (uses from __future__ import annotations).

title: Created lib/claude_executor.py module
content:
**What**: Created a Python module for programmatic Claude Code CLI execution with structured output parsing.
**Why**: Replaces the older Archive agent.py implementation with improvements: no --dangerously-skip-permissions, stream-json parsing, cost tracking, retry with exponential backoff, process group management, safe env filtering.
**Where**: lib/claude_executor.py, lib/__init__.py
**Learned**: Claude stream-json output has 'assistant' messages with usage.input_tokens/output_tokens for cost tracking, and a final 'result' message with session_id and is_error flag. The --allowedTools flag accepts one tool per flag occurrence (repeated). Python 3.9 compat requires Dict[str, str] instead of dict[str, str] and Optional[X] instead of X | None.

title: Created GitHub webhook trigger server
content:
**What**: Created a FastAPI-based GitHub webhook server that receives issue events and launches SDD pipelines automatically via ClaudeExecutor.

**Why**: Enable automated SDD pipeline triggering from GitHub issues — when issues are opened/labeled/commented with trigger keywords, the pipeline runs automatically and posts status updates as issue comments.

**Where**: 
- `lib/webhook_trigger.py` — Main webhook server (FastAPI app)
- `infra/webhook/Dockerfile` — Container image with gh CLI and Claude CLI
- `skills/webhook-trigger/SKILL.md` — Skill documentation
- `requirements.txt` — Added fastapi>=0.100, uvicorn>=0.23

**Learned**:
- Trigger keywords: `[sdd-auto]`, `[ai-workflow]`, `@luum-bot`
- Bot loop prevention uses HTML comment marker `<!-- luum-bot -->`
- Issue classification priority: labels -> /classify_issue body command -> title heuristics -> default feature
- HMAC-SHA256 validation is optional (warns if secret not set, for dev convenience)
- Pipeline runs in daemon thread to avoid blocking webhook response
- Uses `gh issue comment` CLI for posting status updates (requires GH_TOKEN)

title: Created SDD batch runner with ClaudeExecutor integration
content:
**What**: Created `lib/batch_runner.py` (SDD batch runner) and `skills/batch-runner/SKILL.md` (skill docs). The batch runner integrates with the existing `lib/claude_executor.py` (ClaudeExecutor + ClaudeResult).

**Why**: Need to run multiple SDD changes sequentially with timing, cost tracking, JSON reports for CI/CD, and failure resilience.

**Where**: `lib/batch_runner.py`, `lib/__init__.py`, `skills/batch-runner/SKILL.md`

**Learned**: 
- ClaudeExecutor uses `ClaudeResult` (not `ExecutionResult`), with `.run(prompt)` as the main API
- ClaudeExecutor uses `-p` flag (not `--print`), `--output-format stream-json`, and `--verbose`
- ClaudeResult has `.success`, `.duration_secs`, `.tokens_in`, `.tokens_out`, `.cost_usd`, `.error_message`
- The executor handles env filtering, process groups, retry with backoff, and cost estimation internally
- Batch runner supports: --fast-forward, --phase, --batch (YAML), --dry-run, --continue-on-failure, --json-report, --model, --timeout, --verbose
- Python 3.9+ compatible (uses % formatting instead of f-strings for broader compat)

title: Domain-specific evaluator routing for SDD verify
content:
**What**: Created a domain-specific evaluator routing system for SDD verify. The system analyzes affected file paths to classify the domain of a change (backend, frontend, infrastructure, database, security, api, mixed) and provides domain-specific evaluation criteria and focus areas.

**Why**: sdd-verify was using generic evaluation criteria regardless of the type of change. Backend changes need architecture compliance checks, security changes need OWASP review, database changes need migration safety checks, etc. Domain-specific routing ensures the verify phase focuses on what matters most for each type of change.

**Where**:
- `lib/domain_router.py` — core routing logic with detect_domain(), get_evaluator_config(), format_verify_context()
- `customizations/sdd-verify-domains.yaml` — agent customization override with per-domain prompt instructions
- `tests/unit/test_domain_router.py` — 56 unit tests covering all domains and edge cases

**Learned**:
- Path pattern matching needs to prepend "/" to handle relative paths (e.g., "internal/users/service.go" needs to match "/internal/" pattern)
- Security domain gets priority over majority vote — any security-related files should trigger security-focused evaluation
- The customization YAML format follows the agent-customization rule with a `domain_routing` section that maps domains to custom_instructions and model overrides (security gets opus for deeper reasoning)

title: Created issue-to-PR pipeline
content:
**What**: Created `lib/issue_pipeline.py` and `skills/issue-pipeline/SKILL.md` — a full issue-to-PR pipeline that fetches GitHub issues, classifies them, creates worktrees, runs SDD, and opens PRs.
**Why**: Automate the complete lifecycle from GitHub issue to pull request using the SDD pipeline.
**Where**: `lib/issue_pipeline.py`, `skills/issue-pipeline/SKILL.md`
**Learned**: The current `ClaudeExecutor` in `lib/claude_executor.py` uses `ClaudeResult` (not `ExecutionResult` from the archive), constructor takes `working_dir` (not `project_dir`), and the `run()` method returns `ClaudeResult` with `.duration_secs` and `.error_message` fields. The archived ADW pipeline code uses different patterns that needed adaptation. The `ClaudeResult` dataclass does not have `.phase` or `.change_name` fields — those are tracked externally via enumeration indices.

title: Created Codebase Singularity controller (MAPE-K loop)
content:
**What**: Created the Codebase Singularity controller -- the central MAPE-K autonomous loop that ties all Cognitive OS capabilities together.

**Why**: Need a single controller that monitors codebase health events (GitHub issues, test failures, stale docs, KPI degradation, skill failures, coverage drops, circuit breaker states), classifies them, and routes them to the appropriate pipeline (auto-repair, self-improve, doc-sync, metrics-calibrator, coverage-enforcement, skill-creator, issue-to-pr).

**Where**:
- `lib/singularity.py` -- Main controller with SingularityController class, 7 monitors, MAPE-K cycle, daemon mode, CLI
- `skills/singularity/SKILL.md` -- Skill definition for `/singularity` command
- `rules/singularity.md` -- Safety boundaries, phase-dependent behavior, budget limits
- `docs/singularity.md` -- Full documentation of the system

**Learned**:
- Circuit breaker events ALWAYS escalate to human, never auto-acted on
- Uses ClaudeExecutor from lib/claude_executor.py (not the batch_runner version which has a different API)
- Event dedup via MD5 hash of type+source+detail, persisted in singularity-events.jsonl
- Cooldown of 1 hour per event type prevents thrashing
- Budget enforcement reads from metrics/cost-events.jsonl and checks against daily_alert_usd
- Phase gating: production/maintenance restricts to safe event types only (test_failure, stale_docs, kpi_degradation, coverage_drop)
- Python 3.9 compatible: uses typing imports (Optional, List, Dict, Set, Tuple, Any)

title: Created comprehensive user-facing documentation (FAQ, Getting Started, Architecture)
content:
**What**: Created 3 new documentation files and updated INDEX.md for user-facing Cognitive OS documentation.
**Why**: Users asking natural questions during development need answers in structured documentation format.
**Where**: docs/faq.md (FAQ with 25+ questions), docs/getting-started.md (installation to first pipeline), docs/architecture.md (system diagram, MAPE-K, data flow, component inventory), docs/INDEX.md (updated with new docs, removed external source attributions)
**Learned**: All patterns (PITER, ADW, closed-loop prompts, agent experts, leverage points, ZTE) are presented as native Cognitive OS concepts. External attribution references (IndyDevDan, agenticengineer.com, TAC) were removed from INDEX.md. The "Tactical Agentic Coding" section was renamed to "Core Patterns".

title: Comprehensive pytest tests for Singularity MAPE-K controller
content:
**What**: Created 92 comprehensive pytest tests for the Singularity controller covering all MAPE-K phases
**Why**: User requested full test coverage for the singularity controller at lib/singularity.py
**Where**: tests/behavior/test_singularity.py
**Learned**: 
- The singularity module imports `notifications.send_raw` and `claude_executor.ClaudeExecutor` as sibling imports via sys.path manipulation, so tests must mock `notifications` module before importing singularity
- The module uses two metrics directories (_METRICS_DIR and _COGNITIVE_OS_METRICS) as fallback locations, both need patching in tests
- Circuit breaker detection looks in both .cognitive-os/metrics/circuit-breaker/ and metrics/circuit-breaker/
- SingularityController.__init__ calls _load_processed_keys which reads _SINGULARITY_LOG, so for integration tests using __new__ + manual attribute setup avoids filesystem side effects
- SIGTERM handler test works by sending os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGTERM) after _setup_signal_handlers()

title: Audit fixes: CATALOG, RULES-COMPACT, env.example, docker-compose, test paths
content:
**What**: Fixed 5 audit issues: (1) Added 13 missing skills to CATALOG.md, (2) Added 4 missing rules to RULES-COMPACT.md + fixed metrics-calibration reference format, (3) Added 10 missing env vars to env.example, (4) Added webhook-trigger service to docker-compose under automation profile, (5) Fixed test paths in test_config.py (cognitive-os.yaml is at project root, not .cognitive-os/) and test_rules.py (rules/ is at project root, not .cognitive-os/rules/).
**Why**: Audit revealed drift between actual skills/rules on disk and their catalog/compact references, missing env vars that docker-compose requires, and test paths pointing to wrong directories.
**Where**: skills/CATALOG.md, rules/RULES-COMPACT.md, env.example, docker-compose.cognitive-os.yml, tests/system/test_config.py, tests/system/test_rules.py
**Learned**: In luum-agent-os, cognitive-os.yaml lives at project root (not under .cognitive-os/), and rules/ is also at project root. The self-install.sh hook symlinks rules into .claude/rules/ for Claude Code consumption, but tests should reference the source location. LANGFUSE_ENCRYPTION_KEY is required (uses ?Set syntax in docker-compose) -- the service will fail without it.

title: Self-install.sh SYNC_DIRS registry for auto-detection
content:
**What**: Refactored self-install.sh from hardcoded sync calls to a declarative SYNC_DIRS registry array. Each entry is `"src_dir|dest_base|strategy|pattern"`. Adding a new dir to sync requires adding one line.

**Why**: The hook was missing agents/, customizations/, and docs/ directories. Every new directory required manual code changes in multiple places.

**Where**: hooks/self-install.sh, tests/behavior/test_self_install.py

**Learned**:
- Seven dirs are synced: rules (flat->claude), skills (tree->cos), squads (flat->cos), templates (flat->cos), agents (flat->cos), customizations (flat->cos), docs (tree->cos)
- lib/ is NOT synced — it's Python code used directly, not a framework component to symlink
- The tree strategy syncs both subdirectories and top-level files (not just *.md as before — now syncs ALL top-level files)
- Status line now reports: rules, hooks, skills, squads, agents, docs counts
- 50 tests cover all new dirs including TestAgentsSync, TestCustomizationsSync, TestDocsSync, TestAutoDiscovery

title: Fixed corrupt JSONL metrics files (jq multi-line bug)
content:
**What**: Cleaned 7 corrupt JSONL files in .cognitive-os/metrics/ that had multi-line JSON objects (from jq -n bug).
**Why**: JSONL files require one JSON object per line. Multi-line pretty-printed JSON broke parsing.
**Where**: .cognitive-os/metrics/hook-health.jsonl (recovered 25 objects from 2595 lines), capability-snapshots.jsonl (8 objects from 48 lines), knowledge-graph.jsonl (545 objects from 2965 lines), kpi-history.jsonl (was empty in git, 0 objects), 3 arena JSONL files (2 objects each from 30 lines each).
**Learned**: Used brace-depth parsing to recover objects from multi-line JSON. Simple line-by-line concatenation failed for hook-health and kpi-history because their JSON objects contained nested braces. The brace-depth approach (tracking { and } depth to find complete objects) worked reliably.

title: Fixed 40 test failures from sys.modules pollution and docker compose env vars
content:
**What**: Fixed 40 failing tests across 3 root causes:
1. (32 failures) test_notifications.py - sys.modules pollution from test_singularity.py
2. (7 failures) test_service_health.py - docker compose config failing due to missing env vars
3. (1 failure) test_metrics.py - JSONL validation on runtime data (self-resolved)

**Why**: tests/behavior/test_singularity.py used `sys.modules.setdefault("notifications", mock)` at module level which permanently replaced the notifications module for all subsequent tests. Since pytest collects alphabetically (behavior < unit), the mock was inserted before test_notifications.py ran.

**Where**: 
- tests/behavior/test_singularity.py - save/restore sys.modules after importing singularity
- tests/integration/test_service_health.py - skip gracefully when compose config fails due to missing env vars

**Learned**: 
- `sys.modules.setdefault()` for test mocking is dangerous - it permanently pollutes the module cache across test files
- Always save/restore sys.modules when mocking at module level for import interception
- Docker compose tests should skip when env vars required by the compose file are not set

title: Rewrote docs/faq.md as comprehensive "wow effect" FAQ
content:
**What**: Complete rewrite of docs/faq.md from 55 questions to 65 questions, restructured into 10 sections covering every capability of the platform.

**Why**: User requested a FAQ that generates a "wow" effect, covering every piece of the platform comprehensively, structured as "a pitch deck disguised as technical documentation."

**Where**: docs/faq.md

**Learned**: Actual counts from codebase scan: 970 test functions across 52 files (with parametrize expanding runtime count further), 46 rules, 50 hooks, 64 skills, 10 lib modules, 18 Docker services (excluding data volumes and network). The Singularity controller alone has 92 behavior tests. The self-install hook has 50 tests. Contract drift has 54 tests. These real numbers are more impressive than the previous documentation's estimates.

title: Created roadmap.md with 4-phase feature roadmap
content:
**What**: Created docs/roadmap.md with a 4-phase future features roadmap and updated docs/INDEX.md to include it.
**Why**: User requested a structured roadmap document covering multi-model support, web dashboard, enterprise features, and community ecosystem.
**Where**: docs/roadmap.md (new), docs/INDEX.md (updated)
**Learned**: The project already has LiteLLM in the Docker stack, model-routing rules, cost tracking per model, and an identity stack design -- all of which are foundations for roadmap features. The roadmap references these existing components as dependencies rather than greenfield work.

title: Safety mesh: 3 new hooks + rules + tests
content:
**What**: Created 3 safety mesh features: clarification-gate (PreToolUse, BLOCKING for score >60), blast-radius (PreToolUse, advisory estimation), assumption-tracker (PostToolUse, warns on 3+ assumptions)
**Why**: User requested safety features to prevent vague prompts, estimate impact, and track when agents guess instead of working from specs
**Where**: hooks/clarification-gate.sh, hooks/blast-radius.sh, hooks/assumption-tracker.sh, rules/clarification-gate.md, rules/blast-radius.md, rules/assumption-tracking.md, tests/behavior/test_safety_mesh.py, rules/RULES-COMPACT.md
**Learned**: clarification-gate is the only BLOCKING hook (exit 2) among the three. blast-radius and assumption-tracker are advisory (exit 0). All hooks follow the standard pattern: source _lib/safe-jsonl.sh, session-aware metrics, jq-based JSON processing. The scoring for clarification-gate: no file paths +15, scope without counts +20, missing tech +15, action without targets +20, unanswered questions +15, short prompt +20, no criteria +10. "do it" only scores 45 (WARN), need multiple signals to reach BLOCK threshold.

title: Split-and-Resume pattern + safety mesh integration tests
content:
**What**: Implemented the Split-and-Resume pattern — sub-agents can signal NEEDS_CLARIFICATION: during execution, and the orchestrator resolves ambiguity via Engram search or user interaction. Also created 44 integration tests for the safety mesh.

**Why**: Agents sometimes discover ambiguity mid-task that the clarification-gate (pre-launch) cannot catch. Rather than making wrong assumptions, agents can now ask for help.

**Where**:
- rules/split-and-resume.md — full rule definition
- hooks/clarification-interceptor.sh — PostToolUse hook that detects NEEDS_CLARIFICATION marker
- templates/agent-preamble.md — updated with clarification instructions for agents
- rules/RULES-COMPACT.md — added split-and-resume entry to contextual rules
- tests/behavior/test_safety_mesh_integration.py — 44 tests covering cross-feature cascades + edge cases

**Learned**:
- trust-score-validator.sh reads .tool_result (not .tool_response.result) — test helpers need to provide both fields for compatibility across hooks
- clarification-gate scoring: "fix it" alone scores 45 (below 60 block threshold). Need scope words like "all" or action patterns like "add auth" to trigger blocking
- The hook uses grep -P on some systems but needs sed fallback for macOS portability

title: Safety Mesh features 4-7 implementation
content:
**What**: Implemented 4 safety features: Dry-Run Preview, Auto-Rollback, Change Impact Analysis, and Confidence Gate
**Why**: Extend the safety mesh with preview capabilities, automatic rollback on verify failure, change impact analysis, and confidence-based gating
**Where**:
- hooks/dry-run-preview.sh — PreToolUse hook, blocks Agent when DRY_RUN=true
- hooks/auto-rollback-trigger.sh — PostToolUse hook, detects verify-apply exhaustion
- hooks/confidence-gate.sh — PostToolUse hook, blocks low-confidence results in prod/maintenance
- lib/impact_analysis.py — Python module for analyzing change blast radius
- skills/auto-rollback/SKILL.md — Rollback skill for reverting failed sdd-apply commits
- skills/impact-analysis/SKILL.md — Skill wrapper for impact analysis
- rules/dry-run.md, auto-rollback.md, impact-analysis.md, confidence-gate.md — Rule files
- tests/behavior/test_safety_mesh_2.py — 42 behavior tests, all passing
- rules/RULES-COMPACT.md — Updated with all 4 entries
**Learned**:
- RiskLevel enum needed custom __lt__/__gt__ because str Enum comparison is alphabetical, not semantic
- Hooks log to session-scoped metrics dir (via _resolve_metrics_dir) not global metrics dir — tests must check both paths
- safe-jsonl.sh _resolve_metrics_dir uses COGNITIVE_OS_PROJECT_DIR first, then git rev-parse

title: Created E2E smoke test suite for Cognitive OS
content:
**What**: Created comprehensive end-to-end smoke test at `tests/smoke/run-smoke.py` with 5 phases (16+ tests) and a skill at `skills/smoke-test/SKILL.md`.
**Why**: Unit tests validate individual modules but don't validate the real system works end-to-end. This smoke test validates hooks actually execute, config parses, Python libs import, safety mesh hooks block/pass correctly, SDD pipeline logic enforces dependencies, Docker services start, and Claude CLI integration works.
**Where**: `tests/smoke/run-smoke.py`, `skills/smoke-test/SKILL.md`
**Learned**: All 16 core tests pass (phases 1-3). The hooks (clarification-gate, blast-radius, assumption-tracker, dry-run-preview) all work correctly via stdin JSON piping. Phase 4 (Docker) and Phase 5 (Claude CLI) are optional and don't affect exit code. The script uses only stdlib (no pytest dependency). Run with `python3 tests/smoke/run-smoke.py` for default, `--quick` for phases 1-2, `--docker` for phases 1-4, `--all` for all 5.

title: Fixed testcontainers MySQL and ClickHouse integration tests
content:
**What**: Fixed 12 failing integration tests (6 MySQL errors, 6 ClickHouse errors) in test_databases.py
**Why**: testcontainers Python package has no built-in MySqlContainer; wait_for_logs with string predicate is deprecated for ClickHouse
**Where**: tests/integration/test_databases.py
**Learned**:
- testcontainers-python does NOT ship a MySqlContainer module. Use DockerContainer with mysql:8.4 image, manual port mapping, and env vars.
- MySQL accepts TCP connections before user/db initialization is complete. Must retry actual MySQL handshake (mysql.connector.connect) in a loop, not just wait for the port.
- ClickHouse wait_for_logs("Ready for connections") times out because the log pattern doesn't match. Use _wait_for_port + HTTP readiness check (SELECT 1 via urllib) instead.
- ClickHouse HTTP interface requires POST for multi-line DDL/DML queries. GET with urlencode mangles newlines causing 500 errors.
- ClickHouse round(avg(0.42, 0.91), 2) = 0.66 (banker's rounding on 0.665), not 0.67. Use wider tolerance in assertions.
- urllib.request works well as a zero-dependency replacement for requests in ClickHouse HTTP tests.

title: Created infra-health SessionStart hook
content:
**What**: Created `hooks/infra-health.sh` SessionStart hook that auto-detects Docker status and required infrastructure services.
**Why**: Need automated infrastructure health checks at session start to catch missing services early and optionally auto-start them.
**Where**: `hooks/infra-health.sh`, `rules/infra-health.md`, `rules/RULES-COMPACT.md`, `tests/behavior/test_safety_mesh.py`, `.claude/settings.json`, `env.example`
**Learned**: The hook reads services from `cognitive-os.yaml -> resources.infrastructure.services` and compares against `docker compose ps`. Uses `INFRA_AUTO_START` env var (default false) for safe opt-in auto-start. Docker compose profiles are detected per-service. The existing `cognitive-os-health.sh` already does a simpler Docker check -- the new hook provides more detailed per-service reporting and auto-start capability.

title: Rewrote coverage-report.sh to scan Python test files
content:
**What**: Rewrote `tests/coverage-report.sh` to search Python `.py` test files instead of `.sh` test files
**Why**: All tests were migrated from bash to Python but the coverage report still searched for `tests/**/test-*.sh`, resulting in 0% coverage
**Where**: `tests/coverage-report.sh`
**Learned**: The key change was the `tests_referencing()` function — it now greps all `*.py` files under `tests/` instead of specific `test-*.sh` patterns. A `flexible_pattern()` helper converts hyphens to `[-_]` character classes so both `circuit-breaker` and `circuit_breaker` match. After the fix, composite coverage reports 93% (123/132 items covered).

title: Fixed webhook_trigger.py broken API + registered 9 safety mesh hooks
content:
**What**: Fixed webhook_trigger.py to use correct ClaudeExecutor API and registered 9 unregistered safety mesh hooks in settings.json.

**Why**: webhook_trigger.py was using wrong constructor params (project_dir, claude_bin, timeout_seconds instead of working_dir, claude_path, default_timeout), calling non-existent method run_phase() instead of slash(), and referencing result.stderr instead of result.error_message. Also 9 hooks existed as files but were never registered.

**Where**: 
- lib/webhook_trigger.py -- fixed constructor params, method call (slash instead of run_phase), result field (error_message instead of stderr)
- .claude/settings.json -- added 9 hooks: blast-radius, clarification-gate, dry-run-preview, pre-compaction-flush (PreToolUse Agent), assumption-tracker, auto-rollback-trigger, clarification-interceptor, confidence-gate (PostToolUse Agent), sync-to-repo (Stop)

**Learned**: ClaudeExecutor API: constructor takes working_dir/claude_path/default_timeout, methods are run(prompt) and slash(command, args), returns ClaudeResult with error_message field (no stderr). Hook count went from 90 to 108 (9 new + some formatting).

title: Component Registry System — implementation complete
content:
**What**: Created a component registry system to prevent redundancy: overlap-check hook, component-hygiene rule, component-registry.sh CLI, 21 behavior tests, and CONCERNS tags on all 51 hooks.

**Why**: Hooks, rules, and skills were growing without overlap detection. Components with shared concerns could duplicate functionality without anyone noticing.

**Where**:
- hooks/component-overlap-check.sh — PreToolUse on Edit|Write, warns on 2+ shared concerns
- rules/component-hygiene.md — max sizes, mandatory tagging, pre-action checks
- scripts/component-registry.sh — CLI with scan/overlaps/deps/orphans/stats subcommands
- tests/behavior/test_component_registry.py — 21 tests covering all functionality
- All 51 hooks tagged with # CONCERNS: lines

**Learned**:
- macOS sed doesn't support \( \| \) grouping in BRE — use chained substitutions instead: sed 's/\.sh$//;s/\.md$//'
- The registry found 173 components (51 hooks, 55 rules, 67 skills) with concern inference working well for untagged components
- Need to manually register component-overlap-check.sh in .claude/settings.json under PreToolUse Edit|Write matcher
- Rules have an aspirational 60-line max but most existing rules exceed it (avg 87 lines) — this is advisory, not blocking

title: Extracted common hook boilerplate to _lib/common.sh
content:
**What**: Created `hooks/_lib/common.sh` with 5 shared functions (require_tool, resolve_session_dir, get_phase, check_private_mode, read_stdin_json/stdin_field) and updated the top 10 most-invoked hooks to use them.

**Why**: Hooks audit found 500+ lines of duplicated boilerplate across 53 hooks. The session resolution block alone was 12 lines repeated 18 times. This refactor centralizes the common patterns.

**Where**: 
- `hooks/_lib/common.sh` (new file)
- Updated: error-learning.sh, auto-repair-dispatcher.sh, result-truncator.sh, tool-loop-detector.sh, auto-verify.sh, dod-gate.sh, auto-refine.sh, trust-score-validator.sh, agent-checkpoint.sh, skill-tracker.sh

**Learned**: 
- The `require_tool` function needs to read stdin JSON to extract tool_name if not in env, so it depends on read_stdin_json
- common.sh must be sourced AFTER safe-jsonl.sh in hooks that use both, since safe-jsonl.sh installs an EXIT trap
- The guard `_COMMON_SH_LOADED` prevents double-loading when multiple _lib files source common.sh
- Only top 10 hooks updated as proof of pattern; remaining 43 hooks can be migrated incrementally

title: Firecrawl evaluation: AGPL-3.0 license BLOCKER
content:
**What**: Evaluated Firecrawl (mendableai/firecrawl) for potential integration into Cognitive OS skills (eval-repo, contract-drift, document-feature, Singularity controller).

**Why**: User requested tool evaluation for web scraping/crawling capabilities to power several Cognitive OS skills.

**Where**: No files affected -- evaluation only, no integration.

**Learned**:
- License: AGPL-3.0 (confirmed from LICENSE file on GitHub). This is a BLOCKER per rules/license-policy.md. The project's license policy explicitly lists AGPL v3 as blocked for SaaS usage.
- GitHub stats: ~99K stars, 6.6K forks, very active (v2.8.0 released Feb 2025), YC-backed.
- Self-hosting: Docker Compose supported, but self-hosted version lacks advanced features (agent, browser sandbox, enhanced proxies).
- Pricing: Free tier 500 credits, paid from $16/mo (3K credits) to $333/mo (500K credits).
- SDKs are MIT-licensed (only the core server is AGPL).
- DeepWiki covers repo documentation use case (read-only, conversational) but does NOT cover arbitrary web scraping. They serve different purposes.
- The AGPL exception clause (running as completely separate container, no source modifications, API-only communication) COULD apply but requires explicit approval per license-policy.md.
- Alternative: Consider using the Firecrawl MCP server as an external service (API calls to cloud) rather than self-hosting, which avoids AGPL trigger. Or evaluate alternatives like Crawl4AI (Apache 2.0) or Jina Reader API.

**Verdict**: HOLD -- AGPL blocks direct integration/self-hosting. Cloud API usage is viable but adds external dependency and cost. Recommend evaluating Apache-2.0 alternatives first (Crawl4AI, Jina Reader).

title: Hook consolidation: 5 hooks merged into 2
content:
**What**: Merged 5 PostToolUse hooks into 2 to reduce process spawns and stdin reads:
- error-learning.sh + auto-repair-dispatcher.sh -> error-pipeline.sh (PostToolUse Bash)
- auto-verify.sh + dod-gate.sh + auto-refine.sh -> completion-gate.sh (PostToolUse Agent)
Also merged 4 rules: cost-tracking->resource-governance, secret-hygiene->credential-management, metrics-calibration->agent-kpis, sre-protocol->auto-repair. Cleaned phantom Docker services in cognitive-os.yaml (deepeval/ragas/promptfoo marked as planned, automaker/jupyter added).

**Why**: Performance optimization - each PostToolUse hook spawns a separate process and reads stdin independently. Merging hooks that process the same input reduces overhead.

**Where**: hooks/error-pipeline.sh, hooks/completion-gate.sh, hooks/_archived/, .claude/settings.json, cognitive-os.yaml, rules/resource-governance.md, rules/credential-management.md, rules/agent-kpis.md, rules/auto-repair.md, rules/RULES-COMPACT.md

**Learned**: The Write tool and rm commands silently fail when the Bash shell cwd resets between calls. Always use absolute paths or verify file existence in the same command chain. Hooks that run PostToolUse on Edit/Write can interfere with file creation.

title: Library evaluation: Crawl4AI vs Jina Reader (Firecrawl alternative)
content:
**What**: Evaluated Crawl4AI (unclecode/crawl4ai) and Jina Reader API as Firecrawl alternatives for web crawling in Cognitive OS. Firecrawl was rejected due to AGPL license.

**Why**: Need a permissively-licensed web crawler for eval-repo (DeepWiki), contract-drift (API docs), document-feature, and general agent web research.

**Where**: lib/ (Python), skills that need web crawling

**Learned**:
- Crawl4AI: Apache-2.0 license (with attribution clause), 62.7k GitHub stars, v0.8.6 (March 2026), very active. Python async API via AsyncWebCrawler. Supports: JS rendering (Playwright), markdown output, CSS/XPath structured extraction, LLM-based extraction with Pydantic schemas, anti-bot detection, Docker self-hosting (port 11235), REST API, multi-URL concurrent crawling. Python >=3.10. pip install crawl4ai.
- Jina Reader: Apache-2.0 license, 10.4k stars. Primarily a hosted API (r.jina.ai prefix). Self-hostable but designed as a service, not a Python library. Good for simple URL-to-markdown but less flexible for structured extraction or deep crawling.
- Verdict: ADOPT Crawl4AI. It covers all use cases, is Apache-2.0, has massive community, excellent Python API that fits our lib/ layer, and can be self-hosted via Docker. Jina Reader is ASSESS - useful as a lightweight fallback but not primary.
- Attribution requirement: must include "This product includes software developed by UncleCode as part of the Crawl4AI project" in README/NOTICE.

title: Evaluated InsForge - AI-native backend platform
content:
**What**: Evaluated InsForge (github.com/InsForge/InsForge) for potential integration with Cognitive OS.
**Why**: Tool evaluation request for luum-agent-os capabilities enhancement.
**Where**: External tool - no files affected.
**Learned**:
- InsForge is an AI-native backend platform (Supabase alternative) providing Auth, PostgreSQL, S3 storage, Edge Functions, Model Gateway, Site Deployment
- License: Apache 2.0 (SAFE)
- GitHub: 5.1k stars, 533 forks, 3,249 commits, v2.0.1 released March 9 2026
- Has MCP server (insforge-mcp) for Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf integration
- Self-hostable via Docker Compose
- Verdict: HOLD - It solves a different problem (backend-as-a-service for app development). Cognitive OS is an agent orchestration framework, not an app development tool. InsForge would be relevant if Cognitive OS needed to provision backend infrastructure for generated apps, but that is not a current capability or identified gap. No overlap with existing capabilities (Engram, SDD, SRE, skills). Low relevance score.

title: Evaluated Parlant (emcie-co) for Cognitive OS integration
content:
**What**: Evaluated Parlant (github.com/emcie-co/parlant) — a conversational control layer for customer-facing AI agents. Apache 2.0 license, 17.9k stars, 1.5k forks, 47 contributors, Python/FastAPI/MongoDB stack, v3.3.0 as of March 2026.

**Why**: User requested evaluation for potential integration into Cognitive OS agent governance layer.

**Where**: External tool evaluation — no files changed.

**Learned**:
- Parlant is a context-engineering framework for B2C/B2B conversational agents with deterministic behavioral guidelines
- It operates as middleware between apps and LLMs, evaluating conversations turn-by-turn against business rules
- Core concepts: Guidelines (condition-action pairs), Journeys (multi-turn SOPs), Canned Responses, Glossary, Explainability (OpenTelemetry)
- Requires MongoDB for persistence — architectural mismatch with Cognitive OS (which uses SQLite/Engram)
- Designed for customer-facing chatbots, NOT for coding agent orchestration — fundamentally different use case
- License is Apache 2.0 (SAFE) but VC-backed single-company risk of future relicensing
- Our existing rules system (45 rules, hooks, phase-aware enforcement) already covers behavioral control for coding agents more directly
- Verdict: HOLD — interesting architecture but wrong problem domain. Monitor for ideas, do not integrate.

title: Integrated Crawl4AI web crawler
content:
**What**: Integrated Crawl4AI (Apache 2.0) as the web crawling library for Cognitive OS via lib/web_crawler.py wrapper.
**Why**: Evaluated and approved for ADOPT. Provides LLM-ready markdown from web pages with headless browser rendering, structured extraction, and multi-page crawling.
**Where**: lib/web_crawler.py (wrapper module), skills/web-crawler/SKILL.md (skill doc), tests/unit/test_web_crawler.py (26 tests), requirements.txt (crawl4ai>=0.8.0), docs/tool-stack.md (section 11), docs/faq.md (web crawling Q&A), NOTICE (attribution)
**Learned**: The module gracefully degrades -- fetch_markdown falls back to urllib+HTML stripping when crawl4ai is absent, but fetch_structured and crawl_site raise RuntimeError (no meaningful fallback). Tests use asyncio.run() wrapper instead of pytest-asyncio marks since the project doesn't configure asyncio_mode.

title: Implemented 3 features: pre-commit gate, capability levels, model router
content:
**What**: Implemented three new features: (1) pre-commit coverage gate hook, (2) model capability levels with auto-disable, (3) dynamic multi-provider model routing.

**Why**: Pre-commit gate prevents committing with failing tests. Capability levels allow auto-disabling unnecessary safety nets for high-capability models. Model router enables intelligent multi-provider model selection based on task requirements, budget, and local preference.

**Where**:
- hooks/pre-commit-gate.sh, scripts/install-pre-commit.sh, rules/pre-commit-gate.md
- lib/capability_levels.py, rules/capability-levels.md, hooks/_lib/common.sh (check_capability_level function)
- lib/model_router.py, rules/model-routing.md (updated with multi-provider section)
- cognitive-os.yaml (added model_capability section)
- tests/unit/test_capability_levels.py (25 tests), tests/unit/test_model_router.py (43 tests)
- tests/behavior/test_safety_mesh.py (6 new pre-commit gate tests)

**Learned**: The capability levels system uses cumulative disabling — level 4 inherits all disabled components from level 3. The model router supports 8 models across 4 providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, DeepSeek) plus local models (llama, qwen). All 74 new tests pass.

title: Strix evaluation for Cognitive OS integration
content:
**What**: Evaluated usestrix/strix (https://github.com/usestrix/strix) for potential integration with Cognitive OS.

**Why**: User requested evaluation to fill security scanning gaps in Cognitive OS.

**Where**: N/A (external tool evaluation, no files changed)

**Learned**:
- Strix is an autonomous AI pentesting tool (Apache 2.0 license, ~22K GitHub stars, Python-based, last release v0.8.3 March 2026)
- It tests DEPLOYED APPLICATIONS and APIs for vulnerabilities (IDOR, SQLi, XSS, SSRF, JWT flaws, race conditions, etc.)
- It does NOT test for prompt injection in AI agent systems, skill files, or hook integrity
- It requires a running HTTP target to test against -- it's a dynamic application security testing (DAST) tool
- It does NOT do static code analysis of generated code for vulnerability patterns
- Verdict: HOLD -- wrong tool for Cognitive OS's security needs. Strix tests web apps, not AI agent frameworks. Our gaps are: prompt injection in skills, malicious hook detection, generated code vulnerability scanning. Strix addresses none of these. Could be useful for projects BUILT with Cognitive OS that expose web APIs, but that's a project-level concern, not an OS-level one.
- Better alternatives to investigate: Promptfoo (LLM I/O security testing, prompt injection detection), Semgrep/CodeQL (static analysis of generated code), custom hook integrity checks

title: COS coexistence: namespaced rules + settings merge
content:
**What**: Made Cognitive OS coexist safely with existing project `.claude/` configurations. Rules are now namespaced under `.claude/rules/cos/` instead of flat in `.claude/rules/`. Settings.json is merged (not overwritten) via `scripts/merge-settings.sh`. Old flat symlinks are auto-migrated.

**Why**: Previously, installing COS into a project that already had `.claude/` config would overwrite settings.json (losing project hooks) and mix COS rules flat with project rules (causing confusion and potential conflicts).

**Where**: 
- `hooks/self-install.sh` - changed SYNC_DIRS to use `claude_cos` dest type, added migration step, updated rule count path
- `scripts/merge-settings.sh` - new helper for idempotent JSON merge of hook arrays via jq
- `install.sh` - added pre-install conflict detection report, settings merge, namespaced rule copy
- `tests/behavior/test_coexistence.py` - 20 new tests covering namespacing, migration, merge, idempotency
- `tests/behavior/test_self_install.py` - updated 5 tests for new cos/ path
- `docs/getting-started.md` - documented coexistence model and uninstall steps

**Learned**: 
- The `resolve_dest` function needed a new `claude_cos` case that maps `rules` to `.claude/rules/cos/` (not just `.claude/rules/`)
- Migration of old flat symlinks must check `readlink` target to only remove symlinks pointing to our `rules/` dir, never project files or external symlinks
- jq `-s` (slurp) mode with `reduce` handles the multi-level hook merge well (lifecycle events -> matcher groups -> hooks array)

title: OpenRouter como fallback provider — 3 mejoras al model routing
content:
**What**: Integrar OpenRouter como provider de fallback en el model routing de Cognitive OS.

**Why**: Video viral de Álvaro Morales muestra que la gente se queda sin cuota de Claude Code y configura OpenRouter manualmente con hacks como `openrouter/free`. Cognitive OS puede resolver esto automáticamente y de forma inteligente.

**3 mejoras concretas**:

1. **OpenRouter como provider en LiteLLM**: Agregar OpenRouter a la tabla de providers en `model_router.py`. Una sola API key da acceso a 655 modelos (28 gratuitos). LiteLLM ya soporta OpenRouter como provider — solo requiere config.

2. **Modelos gratis como último tier de fallback**: Cuando el budget se agota, en vez de BLOCK total, rutear a `openrouter/free` como degraded mode. El agente sigue funcionando con capacidad reducida pero no se frena. Cadena completa: opus → sonnet → haiku → openrouter/free → BLOCK.

3. **El pain point valida el feature**: La gente paga o hackea manualmente para no quedarse sin cuota. El token economy + cost governance + model routing de Cognitive OS resuelve esto automáticamente — sabe cuándo usar cada modelo según la tarea, no "poné free y rezá".
