**Where**: `lib/model_router.py`, `rules/model-routing.md`, `rules/resource-governance.md`, `cognitive-os.yaml` (providers section)

**Learned**: El segmento de usuarios que busca "Claude Code gratis" es enorme y valida nuestro approach de cost governance. OpenRouter es la forma más fácil de agregar 655 modelos con una sola integración.

title: Added OpenRouter free models to model_router.py
content:
**What**: Added 3 OpenRouter free models to MODEL_CAPABILITIES: `openrouter/free` (auto-selector), `qwen/qwen3-32b:free`, `nvidia/llama-3.1-nemotron-ultra-253b:free`. Updated TASK_CAPABILITY_MAP budget list and select_model to prefer free models when budget <= $0.01.
**Why**: Enables zero-cost model routing as a last-tier fallback before blocking, and provides budget-conscious options via OpenRouter.
**Where**: lib/model_router.py
**Learned**: The select_model function filters free models (cost_per_1m_in == 0 AND cost_per_1m_out == 0) when budget_remaining <= 0.01, before the normal affordable-model filtering runs. This means free OpenRouter models + local models compete on capability scores when budget is exhausted. The nvidia/nemotron model has the best reasoning (6) among free options.

title: install.sh: dual-mode local/remote installation
content:
**What**: Added local install mode to install.sh alongside existing remote (git clone) mode.
**Why**: Users working from a local checkout of luum-agent-os should be able to install without cloning from GitHub. This enables offline installs, development workflows, and faster iteration.
**Where**: install.sh (project root)
**Learned**: 
- Auto-detection checks for hooks/, rules/, skills/ relative to the script's directory (BASH_SOURCE[0])
- --from flag allows explicit path specification with validation
- git prerequisite check is now conditional (only required for remote installs)
- prepare_source() function abstracts the source acquisition, rest of install is source-agnostic
- cp -r replaces the temp dir contents for local mode (rm + cp pattern)

title: Updated installation docs for dual-mode installer
content:
**What**: Updated README.md Quick Start section and CHANGELOG.md to document the dual-mode installer (local auto-detection + --from flag + remote curl|bash).
**Why**: install.sh gained local source auto-detection and --from flag; docs needed to reflect both installation modes.
**Where**: README.md (Quick Start section), CHANGELOG.md (new Unreleased section)
**Learned**: No docs/installation.md exists -- all installation docs live in README.md Quick Start. CHANGELOG uses Keep a Changelog format with [Unreleased] section convention.

title: Smart Infrastructure module created
content:
**What**: Created `lib/smart_infra.py` — the core Smart Infrastructure module for lazy Docker service lifecycle management.
**Why**: Enables on-demand Docker service startup when skills/hooks need them, with idle timeout auto-shutdown to save resources.
**Where**: `lib/smart_infra.py`
**Learned**:
- cognitive-os.yaml uses underscores for service names (nemo_guardrails) while the compose map uses hyphens — _get_service_config handles both.
- Container names follow the pattern `cognitive-os-{service}` (from docker-compose.cognitive-os.yml).
- Services with profiles (memory, observability, ui, automation) need `--profile` flag in docker compose commands.
- The module follows the singleton + convenience functions pattern from other lib/ modules (rate_limiter, model_router).
- JSONL metrics logged to `.cognitive-os/metrics/infra-usage.jsonl` matching the pattern from infra-health rule.
- Graceful degradation everywhere: Docker unavailable, compose file missing, health check failures — all return False, never crash.

title: Tech radar update — 114 repos analyzed from awesome-claude-code
content:
**What**: Analyzed 114 repos from awesome-claude-code. 7 ADOPT, 19 WATCH, 5 BLOCK (AGPL/GPL), 83 SKIP.

**ADOPT**: agnix (linter), claude-code-action (CI/CD), Usage Monitor (cost UI), hcom (agent comms), parry (injection scanner), recall (conversation search), Trail of Bits Skills (security audit).

**BLOCK**: Auto-Claude (AGPL), Claude Squad (AGPL), Claudix (AGPL), Context Engineering Kit (GPL), pre-commit-hooks (AGPL).

**Why**: Each ADOPT fills a gap in Cognitive OS. Each must be documented in tech-radar and integrated with tests.

**Where**: docs/tool-stack.md, docs/blocked-tools.md

**Learned**: ~25% of awesome-list repos are already 404. The ecosystem moves fast. None threatens COS in scope — most are point tools.

title: Claude Code ecosystem tech radar — 31 tools evaluated
content:
**What**: Added section 18 "Claude Code Ecosystem" to docs/tool-stack.md with 26 non-blocked tools (7 ADOPT, 19 WATCH) and added 5 blocked tools to docs/blocked-tools.md.

**Why**: Systematic evaluation of the Claude Code ecosystem to identify integration opportunities and avoid license-blocked tools.

**Where**: docs/tool-stack.md (new section 18), docs/blocked-tools.md (5 new entries + summary table update), CHANGELOG.md (unreleased entry)

**Learned**:
- ADOPT (7): agnix (linter), claude-code-action (CI), Usage Monitor (cost), hcom (hook comms), parry (injection scanner), recall (session search), Trail of Bits Skills (security)
- WATCH (19): Everything Claude Code (113.8K stars largest), SuperClaude (22K), Ruflo/claude-flow (27.7K), Context7 (50.9K), Repomix (22.7K), OpenAI Swarm (21K), plus 13 smaller tools
- BLOCKED (5): Auto-Claude (AGPL), Claude Squad (AGPL), Claudix (AGPL), Context Engineering Kit (GPL), pre-commit-hooks/aRustyDev (AGPL)
- Key gaps filled: agnix for config linting, parry for ML-based prompt injection, recall for raw session search, Trail of Bits for professional security skills
- CC-BY-SA-4.0 (Trail of Bits) is acceptable for skills/documentation content

title: Research: agnix linter for AI agent configs
content:
**What**: agnix is a Rust-based linter for AI agent configuration files (CLAUDE.md, SKILL.md, AGENTS.md, hooks, MCP configs). 342 rules across 36 categories, 102 with auto-fix. Built by avifenesh, 112 GitHub stars.

**Why**: Cognitive OS has 55+ rules, dozens of skills, hooks, and CLAUDE.md files. agnix can validate their correctness structurally, catching broken configs before they silently fail. Vercel research shows skills invoke at 0% without correct syntax.

**Where**: https://github.com/agent-sh/agnix

**Key Facts**:
- License: MIT OR Apache-2.0 (dual license, fully compatible)
- Language: Rust (fast, compiled binary)
- Install: `npm install -g agnix`, `brew install agnix`, `cargo install agnix-cli`
- Config: `.agnix.toml` (TOML format)
- Output formats: text, json, sarif
- Has pre-commit hooks support (`.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` included)
- Has GitHub Action (`agent-sh/agnix@v0`)
- Has LSP server (real-time IDE validation)
- Has MCP server (`agnix-mcp` crate)
- Has WASM bindings (browser playground)

**Rule Categories Relevant to Cognitive OS**:
- CC-SK-* (31 rules): Validate SKILL.md files (frontmatter, name format, tool references)
- CC-HK-* (19 rules): Validate hooks in settings.json (type field, matchers)
- CC-MEM-* (12 rules): Validate CLAUDE.md and .claude/rules/*.md
- CC-AG-* (13 rules): Validate agents/*.md
- MCP-* (24 rules): Validate MCP configs
- AS-* (19 rules): Agent Skills spec validation
- PE-* (6 rules): Prompt engineering quality (lost-in-middle, weak language)
- XP-* (9 rules): Cross-platform conflicts
- XML-* (3 rules): XML tag balance in markdown
- REF-* (4 rules): Import reference validation

**Configuration (.agnix.toml)**:
- `severity`: Warning/Error threshold
- `target`: Tool focus (claude-code, cursor, codex, copilot, generic)
- `tools`: Multi-tool targeting array
- `strict`: Treat warnings as errors
- `disabled_rules`: Array of rule IDs to skip
- `exclude`: Glob patterns to skip (e.g., tests/fixtures/**)
- `[rules]` section: Enable/disable rule categories (skills, hooks, agents, memory, plugins, mcp, etc.)
- `max_files`: Scan limit (default 10000)
- `format`: text/json/sarif
- `locale`: en/es/zh-CN

**Pre-commit hook support**:
Two hooks defined: `agnix` (lint only, --strict) and `agnix-fix` (lint + auto-fix).
Files matched: SKILL.md, CLAUDE.md, CLAUDE.local.md, AGENTS.md, .claude/settings.json, .claude/settings.local.json, .claude/agents/*.md, *.mcp.json, copilot instructions, cursor rules.

**Auto-fix capabilities**: 102 rules have auto-fixes with 3 confidence levels:
- `--fix-safe`: HIGH confidence only
- `--fix`: HIGH + MEDIUM confidence
- `--fix-unsafe`: All fixes including LOW confidence

**Learned**:
- agnix validates the STRUCTURE of agent configs, not their semantic correctness
- It catches: invalid YAML frontmatter, wrong field names, missing required fields, generic instructions, XML tag imbalance, broken import references, hardcoded secrets, platform-specific conflicts
- It does NOT validate: whether rules make sense together, runtime behavior, or custom domain logic
- The `--dry-run --show-fixes` mode previews fixes without applying them
- SARIF output enables integration with GitHub Code Scanning

title: Research: parry-guard prompt injection scanner for Cognitive OS integration
content:
**What**: Researched vaporif/parry-guard — a Rust-based prompt injection scanner designed specifically for Claude Code hooks. MIT licensed, 27 stars, actively maintained (last update 2026-03-26).

**Why**: Evaluating as a security layer to strengthen content-policy.sh and agent-security rules in Cognitive OS.

**Where**: External tool — would integrate via hooks in settings.json (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit)

**Learned**:

## Key Facts
- **License**: MIT (confirmed, fully compatible)
- **Language**: Rust with 7 crates: cli, core, daemon, exfil, hook, ml, destructive
- **Version**: 0.1.3 (early development, alpha)
- **ML Models**: DeBERTa v3 (ProtectAI) + optional Llama Prompt Guard 2 (Meta)
- **Backends**: ONNX (default, fast) or Candle (pure Rust, portable, 5-6x slower)
- **Distribution**: cargo install, prebuilt binaries (macOS/Linux), uvx (Python/maturin wrapper), Nix flake
- **Prerequisite**: HuggingFace account + token (models are gated)

## Detection Layers (6 total, fail-closed)
1. **Unicode**: invisible chars (PUA, unassigned), homoglyphs, RTL overrides
2. **Substring**: Aho-Corasick matching for known injection phrases
3. **Secrets**: 40+ regex patterns (AWS, GitHub, GitLab, cloud providers, DB URIs, private keys)
4. **ML classification**: DeBERTa v3 transformer, 256-char chunks with 25-char overlap, head+tail strategy, threshold 0.7
5. **Bash exfiltration**: tree-sitter AST analysis — network sinks, command substitution, obfuscation (base64/hex/ROT13), DNS tunneling, 60+ sensitive paths, 40+ exfil domains
6. **Script exfiltration**: same source-to-sink analysis across 16 languages

## Scan Modes
- **fast** (default): DeBERTa v3 only, ~10ms short text, ~70ms medium, ~136ms long (ONNX)
- **full**: DeBERTa + Llama Prompt Guard 2, ~1.5s/chunk (candle only, no ONNX export for Llama)
- **custom**: user-defined models via ~/.config/parry-guard/models.toml

## Performance (Apple Silicon, ONNX, fast mode)
- Short text (1 chunk): ~10ms
- Medium text (2 chunks): ~32ms
- Long text (6 chunks): ~136ms
- Cold start (daemon + model load): ~580ms
- Cached result: ~8ms
- Cache: redb database, 30-day TTL, shared across projects

## Hook Architecture
- **PreToolUse**: 7 checks — repo state, taint enforcement, CLAUDE.md scanning, exfil blocking, destructive op detection, sensitive path blocking, input injection scanning
- **PostToolUse**: scans tool output for injection and secrets, auto-taints on detection
- **UserPromptSubmit**: audits .claude/ directory for dangerous permissions, injected commands, hook scripts
- Runs as daemon (auto-starts, 30-min idle timeout)
- Configurable timeouts: PreToolUse 1000ms, PostToolUse 5000ms, UserPromptSubmit 2000ms

## Integration Plan for Cognitive OS
1. **Complements content-policy.sh**: parry handles ML-based injection detection + exfil analysis; content-policy handles term-based policy enforcement. No overlap, pure complement.
2. **Complements agent-security**: parry adds runtime input/output scanning that permission system cannot catch (prompt injection in file content, exfil in bash commands).
3. **Hook placement**: Add as ADDITIONAL hooks alongside existing ones, not replacement. parry runs as separate binary via daemon.
4. **Install method**: Prebuilt binary from GitHub releases (simplest for macOS). Alternative: cargo install or uvx.
5. **Config needed**: HF_TOKEN env var, PARRY_IGNORE_DIRS for trusted paths, threshold tuning.

## Risks
- Early development (v0.1.3, alpha) — false positives expected
- HuggingFace token requirement adds setup friction
- 580ms cold start on first scan per session
- Model download on first run (~200MB+ for DeBERTa)
- Full mode (Llama) requires candle backend, 5-6x slower

## Estimated Effort
- Installation + config: 1 hour
- Hook registration in settings.json: 15 minutes
- Testing with sample injections: 2-3 hours
- Documentation: 1 hour
- Total: ~half a day

title: Research: claude-code-action integration analysis
content:
**What**: Full analysis of anthropics/claude-code-action (v1.0) for integration with Cognitive OS.

**Repository**: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code-action
**License**: MIT (confirmed)
**Stars**: 6,681 | **Language**: TypeScript | **Runtime**: Bun

## Key Facts

### Supported GitHub Events
- `issue_comment` (created) — @claude mentions in PR/issue comments
- `pull_request_review_comment` (created) — @claude in review comments
- `pull_request_review` (submitted) — @claude in PR reviews
- `issues` (opened, assigned, labeled) — new issues, assignment, label triggers
- `pull_request` (opened, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened) — PR automation
- Any workflow_dispatch or schedule — via prompt input (agent mode)

### Two Modes (Auto-Detected)
1. **Tag mode**: Triggered by @claude mentions. Builds full context (PR diff, comments, CI status). Interactive.
2. **Agent mode**: Triggered by `prompt` input. Runs automation tasks (review, triage, code changes). Headless.

### Configuration Options
- `trigger_phrase`: Custom trigger (default: @claude)
- `assignee_trigger`: Trigger on issue assignment
- `label_trigger`: Trigger on label (default: "claude")
- `prompt`: Direct instructions for automation mode
- `claude_args`: Pass CLI args (--model, --max-turns, --allowedTools, --mcp-config, --system-prompt, --json-schema)
- `settings`: Claude Code settings as JSON or file path
- `anthropic_api_key` / `claude_code_oauth_token`: Auth
- `use_bedrock` / `use_vertex` / `use_foundry`: Cloud provider support
- `track_progress`: Visual progress tracking comments
- `use_commit_signing` / `ssh_signing_key`: Signed commits
- `plugins` / `plugin_marketplaces`: Claude Code plugins
- `additional_permissions`: Extra GitHub permissions (actions:read for CI logs)
- `structured_output`: JSON schema validation for automation outputs

### How It Interacts with Claude Code
- Installs Claude Code CLI on the runner
- Executes via base-action SDK (not subprocess)
- Respects repository CLAUDE.md (loaded automatically from repo root)
- Supports .claude/commands/ for custom slash commands
- Supports .mcp.json for MCP server configuration
- Supports settings.json for Claude Code configuration
- Full access to Claude Code's tool system (Edit, Read, Write, Bash, etc.)

### Custom CLAUDE.md / Skills Support
- YES: CLAUDE.md is loaded from the repo root automatically
- YES: .claude/commands/ directory works for custom slash commands
- YES: settings input can point to a settings.json file
- YES: MCP servers can be configured via claude_args --mcp-config
- YES: Plugins can be installed from marketplaces

### Outputs
- `execution_file`: Path to execution output
- `branch_name`: Branch created by Claude
- `github_token`: Token used
- `structured_output`: JSON validated against schema
- `session_id`: For --resume continuation

## Integration Plan for Cognitive OS

### Workflow Files to Create

1. **claude-interactive.yml** — Tag mode for @claude mentions on PRs/issues
   - Events: issue_comment, pull_request_review_comment, pull_request_review, issues
   - Uses our CLAUDE.md and .claude/rules/ (already in repo via self-install)
   - Permissions: contents:write, pull-requests:write, issues:write, id-token:write, actions:read

2. **claude-pr-review.yml** — Automatic PR review on every PR
   - Events: pull_request (opened, synchronize)
   - Custom prompt with Cognitive OS quality gates focus
   - --allowedTools scoped to review tools only (no Bash)

3. **claude-issue-triage.yml** — Auto-triage new issues
   - Events: issues (opened)
   - Structured output for label classification
   - Label + priority assignment

4. **claude-ci-fix.yml** — Auto-fix CI failures
   - Events: workflow_run (completed with failure)
   - Prompt to analyze and fix failing tests/lint
   - additional_permissions: actions:read

### How It Complements Existing Workflows
- `ci.yml` (existing): Runs tests, lint, docker validation, security checks
- claude-code-action adds: AI-powered PR review, interactive @claude, issue triage, CI failure auto-fix
- The action respects our CLAUDE.md which already contains all Cognitive OS rules
- Our existing rules (adversarial-review, acceptance-criteria, trust-score) guide Claude's behavior in CI

### What Tests We'd Write
1. Validate workflow YAML syntax (already covered by ci.yml YAML validation)
2. Test that CLAUDE.md is accessible from workflow runner context
3. Test structured output schema validation for triage workflow
4. Integration test: create mock issue, verify label assignment
5. Integration test: create mock PR, verify review comment posted

### Estimated Effort
- Trivial-Small complexity (3-4 workflow files, no code changes)
- ~2-3 hours to set up workflows + test
- Requires: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY secret in repo settings
- Optional: GitHub App installation via `claude /install-github-app`

### Risks
- Cost: Each PR review/issue triage consumes API tokens
- Security: claude_args --allowedTools must be scoped carefully
- Rate limits: High-traffic repos may hit API limits
- Our CLAUDE.md is large (~50K tokens of rules) — may increase cost per invocation

### Recommendations
1. Start with claude-interactive.yml (tag mode) — lowest risk, highest value
2. Add PR review for critical paths only (paths filter on hooks/, rules/, skills/)
3. Use --model claude-4-0-sonnet for reviews (cost-effective)
4. Use structured_output for issue triage (reliable label assignment)
5. Configure --max-turns 10 to cap costs

title: Integration plan: agnix into Cognitive OS
content:
**What**: Integration plan for agnix linter into Cognitive OS (luum-agent-os)

**Why**: Structural validation of CLAUDE.md, SKILL.md, hooks, agents, and MCP configs prevents silent config breakage. Complements existing quality gates (content-policy, pre-commit-gate) with agent-config-specific validation.

**Integration Plan**:

## 1. Installation
- Add `agnix` as npm devDependency OR document `npm install -g agnix` in setup
- Alternative: download binary in `self-install.sh` SessionStart hook
- Prefer npm for reproducibility: `npx agnix .`

## 2. Configuration File: `.agnix.toml`
```toml
severity = "Warning"
target = "claude-code"
strict = false
exclude = [
  "node_modules/**",
  ".git/**",
  "tests/fixtures/**",
]

[rules]
skills = true
hooks = true
agents = true
memory = true
plugins = true
mcp = true
generic_instructions = true
frontmatter_validation = true
xml_balance = true
import_references = true

disabled_rules = [
  # Customize based on Cognitive OS conventions that differ from defaults
]
```

## 3. Hook Integration (3 options)

### Option A: PostToolUse hook on Edit|Write (recommended)
- New hook: `hooks/agnix-lint.sh`
- Fires after Edit/Write on agent config files (.md in skills/, rules/, CLAUDE.md, settings.json)
- Runs `agnix --format json .` on changed files
- Parses JSON output for errors
- Advisory in reconstruction, BLOCK in production (like content-policy.sh)
- Performance: agnix is Rust, runs in <500ms typically

### Option B: Pre-commit integration
- Add to `.pre-commit-config.yaml`:
  ```yaml
  - repo: https://github.com/agent-sh/agnix
    rev: v0.x.x
    hooks:
      - id: agnix
  ```
- Runs alongside existing pre-commit-gate.sh

### Option C: CI-only via GitHub Action
- Add to `.github/workflows/ci.yml`:
  ```yaml
  - uses: agent-sh/agnix@v0
    with:
      target: claude-code
      strict: true
  ```

### Recommended: All three (layered defense)
- PostToolUse hook for real-time feedback during development
- Pre-commit for gate before commits
- CI for authoritative validation in PR checks

## 4. cognitive-os.yaml Integration
```yaml
validation:
  agnix:
    enabled: true
    strict: false  # true in production phase
    auto_fix: false  # true only with explicit user approval
    target: claude-code
```

## 5. Tests to Write
- Unit: Validate `.agnix.toml` config is valid
- Integration: Run `agnix .` on the repo and verify exit code 0
- Regression: Ensure all existing SKILL.md files pass validation
- Regression: Ensure CLAUDE.md passes validation
- Regression: Ensure hooks in settings.json pass validation
- Edge: Verify disabled_rules suppress known false positives
- Performance: agnix completes in <2s on full repo

## 6. Estimated Effort
- Config file creation (.agnix.toml): 0.5h
- PostToolUse hook (agnix-lint.sh): 1h
- Pre-commit integration: 0.5h
- CI GitHub Action: 0.5h
- Fix existing validation errors in SKILL.md/CLAUDE.md: 2-4h (depends on how many)
- Tests: 1h
- Documentation in rules/: 0.5h
- **Total: 6-8 hours**

## 7. Risks
- agnix may flag Cognitive OS conventions as violations (e.g., custom frontmatter fields)
  - Mitigation: Use `disabled_rules` for known false positives
- agnix updates may introduce new rules that break CI
  - Mitigation: Pin version in npm/pre-commit/action
- Performance overhead if running on every Edit/Write
  - Mitigation: Only run on agent config file patterns, agnix is fast (Rust)

**Where**: hooks/agnix-lint.sh (new), .agnix.toml (new), cognitive-os.yaml (update validation section), .github/workflows/ci.yml (update)

**Learned**: agnix is the most comprehensive linter for AI agent configs available. 342 rules, Rust performance, multi-format output, auto-fix. Dual MIT/Apache-2.0 license is fully compatible. Pre-commit hooks and GitHub Action are ready to use out of the box.

title: Research: zippoxer/recall — Full-text search for Claude Code sessions
content:
**What**: Deep analysis of github.com/zippoxer/recall for potential integration into Cognitive OS alongside Engram.

**License**: MIT — fully compatible, no restrictions.

**Tech Stack**: Rust (edition 2021), key dependencies:
- tantivy 0.22 (full-text search engine, Rust equivalent of Lucene)
- ratatui 0.29 (TUI framework)
- tokio (async runtime)
- serde/serde_json (JSONL parsing)
- clap 4 (CLI)
- chrono (timestamps)
- walkdir (file discovery)

**How It Works — Architecture**:
1. **Session Discovery** (`parser/mod.rs`): Scans `~/.claude/projects/*/*.jsonl` for Claude Code sessions, also supports Codex (`~/.codex/sessions/`), Factory (`~/.factory/sessions/`), and OpenCode. Skips `agent-*` sidechain files.

2. **JSONL Parsing** (`parser/claude.rs`): Reads each line as JSON, extracts `type` (user/assistant), `sessionId`, `cwd`, `gitBranch`, `timestamp`, and message content. Filters out compaction summaries (`isCompactSummary`), transcript-only messages, meta messages, and slash command expansions. Extracts text blocks from assistant content arrays (skips tool_use, thinking blocks).

3. **Search Index** (`index/schema.rs`): Uses **Tantivy** (Rust full-text search engine) with this schema:
   - session_id (STRING|STORED)
   - source (STRING|STORED) — claude/codex/factory/opencode
   - file_path (STRING|STORED)
   - cwd (STRING|STORED)
   - git_branch (STRING|STORED)
   - timestamp (I64|INDEXED|STORED|FAST) — unix epoch
   - message_index (U64|STORED) — position within session
   - content (TEXT|STORED) — the actual message text

   Each MESSAGE is indexed as a separate document (not per-session). This enables match-recency ranking within sessions.

4. **Incremental Indexing** (`index/state.rs`, `index/sync.rs`): Tracks indexed files in `state.json` (mtime + size per file). Only re-indexes files that changed. Commits every 200 files during indexing. Index stored at `~/.cache/recall/index/`.

5. **Search Ranking**: Uses Tantivy BM25 + phrase boost (10x for exact multi-word matches) + exponential recency decay (7-day half-life). Groups results by session, keeping highest-scoring message per session.

6. **Session Resume**: Generates `claude --resume {id}` commands. Customizable via env vars (RECALL_CLAUDE_CMD).

7. **CLI Mode** (`cli.rs`): `recall search`, `recall list`, `recall read` — all output JSON for agent consumption. Supports `--source`, `--since`, `--until`, `--cwd`, `--context` filters. Human-friendly time parsing ("2 weeks ago", "yesterday").

8. **TUI Mode** (`app.rs`, `tui.rs`, `ui.rs`): Interactive ratatui interface with real-time search, session browsing, message scrolling, and one-key resume.

**Gap Analysis — recall vs Engram**:
| Dimension | Engram | recall |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Data source | Curated observations (decisions, bugs, discoveries) | Raw conversation transcripts |
| Storage | SQLite (WAL mode) | Tantivy index (~/.cache/recall/index/) |
| Search | Keyword search on saved observations | Full-text search on ALL messages |
| Granularity | Summary-level (what we decided) | Message-level (what we said) |
| Resume | No (context only) | Yes (claude --resume {id}) |
| Cross-session | Yes (persistent memory) | Yes (indexes all sessions) |
| Structured data | Yes (title, type, topic_key, project) | No (raw text only) |
| Session metadata | No | Yes (cwd, git_branch, timestamp) |

**Key Gap recall Fills**: Engram stores DECISIONS and SUMMARIES — the distilled knowledge. recall indexes the raw CONVERSATIONS — the full context of how decisions were made, what was discussed, what approaches were tried and rejected. When `mem_search` doesn't find what you need, recall can search the actual conversation history.

**Integration Options**:

Option A — **Companion Tool (RECOMMENDED)**:
- Install recall as external dependency (brew install zippoxer/tap/recall)
- Create a Cognitive OS skill `/recall-search` that wraps `recall search --help`
- Add recall search as fallback in Engram search protocol: mem_search -> mem_get_observation -> recall search
- Minimal effort, maximum value, no maintenance burden

Option B — **Embedded Search Logic**:
- Port Tantivy indexing logic to Python or call recall binary from lib/
- Build a hybrid index: Engram observations + recall raw messages
- Higher effort, more control, maintenance burden of keeping up with recall updates

Option C — **Index Enrichment**:
- Run recall indexing, then extract structured data from matched conversations to auto-populate Engram
- Example: recall finds a conversation about an architecture decision -> extract and save to Engram automatically
- Interesting but complex, risk of noise

**Recommended Approach**: Option A (Companion Tool) with gradual Option C enrichment.

**Tests We'd Write**:
1. Skill trigger test: verify `/recall-search` activates on "find conversation about X"
2. Fallback chain test: mem_search misses -> recall search finds
3. Resume integration test: recall resume command generates valid claude --resume
4. Session filter test: --cwd filter correctly scopes to project directory
5. Index freshness test: verify recall indexes new sessions before searching

**Estimated Effort**:
- Option A: 2-4 hours (skill creation + documentation + fallback chain update)
- Option B: 2-3 days (Tantivy integration, Python bindings or subprocess wrapper)
- Option C: 1-2 days additional on top of A (extraction logic, dedup with existing Engram data)

**Where**: Would create `skills/recall-search/SKILL.md`, update `rules/context-optimization.md` dual-search protocol, update `CATALOG.md`

**Learned**:
- recall skips agent sidechain files (agent-*.jsonl) — only indexes main conversation
- Tantivy 0.22 is a mature Rust search engine, overkill to reimplement
- The CLI JSON output mode is specifically designed for agent consumption — perfect for our use case
- Phrase boost (10x) + recency decay (7-day half-life) is a solid ranking strategy worth noting
- recall is actively maintained (v0.5.0, last update March 2026, 164 stars)

title: Research: hcom (claude-hook-comms) for agent communication integration
content:
**What**: Deep analysis of hcom (github.com/aannoo/claude-hook-comms) v0.7.7 for potential integration into Cognitive OS agent communication layer.

**Repository Facts**:
- Name: hcom (renamed from claude-hook-comms)
- License: MIT (safe)
- Language: Rust binary + Python pip wrapper
- Stars: 170, actively maintained (last update 2026-03-27)
- Install: `curl | sh`, `pip install hcom`, or `uv tool install hcom`

**Architecture**:
- SQLite database at `~/.hcom/hcom.db` (schema v17) for message storage, instance tracking, events
- TCP notification server for instant wake-up (poll-based, no busy-waiting)
- PTY wrapper (`hcom claude`) intercepts hooks via stdin JSON
- MQTT relay for cross-device sync (optional, uses public brokers: emqx, hivemq, mosquitto)
- Rust binary handles all hook types: sessionstart, pre, post, poll, notify, permission-request, subagent-start, subagent-stop, sessionend, userpromptsubmit

**Communication Protocol**:
- Messages stored in SQLite events table with type='message'
- Delivery via cursor-based polling: each instance tracks last_event_id
- TCP notify pings wake sleeping delivery loops instantly (no polling delay)
- Message envelope: intent (request/inform/ack), reply_to, thread, bundle_id
- Scope: broadcast (all) or mentions (targeted @name)
- File collision detection: detects when 2 agents edit same file within 30s
- Max message size capped (MAX_MESSAGE_SIZE constant)

**Key Features**:
1. Cross-terminal agent messaging (Claude, Gemini, Codex, OpenCode)
2. Agent spawning/forking from within agent sessions
3. File edit collision detection and notification
4. Agent status tracking (active, listening, blocked, inactive)
5. TUI dashboard for monitoring all agents
6. Cross-device sync via MQTT relay
7. Transcript recording and archival
8. Bundle operations (group commands across agents)
9. Instance naming/tagging for addressing
10. Resume interrupted sessions

**Comparison with our Valkey pub/sub (lib/agent_bus.py)**:

| Dimension | hcom | Cognitive OS Valkey Bus |
|-----------|------|----------------------|
| Transport | SQLite + TCP notify | Valkey (Redis) pub/sub |
| Dependencies | None (single binary) | Docker container (Valkey) |
| Setup | `pip install hcom` | Docker Compose + env vars |
| Scope | Cross-terminal, cross-tool | Within Claude Code sessions |
| Message types | Free-form + envelope | Structured (heartbeat, progress, question, control) |
| File collision | Built-in (30s window) | Manual via file_lock_registry.py |
| Agent discovery | Auto via hooks + PTY | Manual agent_id parameter |
| Cross-device | MQTT relay (optional) | Not supported |
| TUI | Built-in ratatui dashboard | Python curses dashboard |
| Protocol | SQLite events + TCP wake | Redis pub/sub channels |
| Latency | ~ms (TCP notify) | ~ms (Redis pub/sub) |
| Persistence | SQLite (survives crashes) | Redis (ephemeral by default) |
| Multi-tool | Claude, Gemini, Codex, OpenCode | Claude Code only |

**Integration Assessment**:

COMPLEMENTARY, NOT REPLACEMENT. hcom solves a different problem (cross-terminal, cross-tool agent coordination) while our Valkey bus solves intra-session orchestrator-to-subagent communication. They operate at different layers.

hcom CANNOT replace Valkey bus because:
1. hcom operates at the terminal/session level; our bus operates at the sub-agent level within a single session
2. hcom requires PTY wrapping (`hcom claude`); our bus is embedded in the orchestrator
3. hcom doesn't support our structured protocol (heartbeat intervals, progress markers, clarification Q&A flow)
4. hcom has no concept of our orchestrator pattern (coordinator + delegated sub-agents)

hcom CAN complement Valkey bus by:
1. Enabling cross-terminal coordination when multiple Claude Code sessions work on the same project
2. File collision detection across sessions (our session-concurrency.md uses advisory locks, hcom does real-time detection)
3. Cross-tool support (if we add Gemini CLI or Codex to our workflow)
4. Message persistence (SQLite survives crashes; Valkey is ephemeral)

**Potential Integration Plan**:

Phase 1 (Low effort, ~2 days):
- Add hcom as optional dependency in cognitive-os.yaml
- Create `hooks/hcom-bridge.sh` that forwards relevant events to hcom if installed
- Document in rules/agent-communication.md as optional cross-terminal layer
- Config: `agent_communication.hcom.enabled: false` (opt-in)

Phase 2 (Medium effort, ~3 days):
- Create `lib/hcom_bridge.py` that wraps hcom CLI for programmatic use
- Bridge file collision events into our session-concurrency advisory lock system
- Forward hcom messages to Engram for cross-session context
- Add hcom status to `/cognitive-os-status` health check

Phase 3 (Optional, ~2 days):
- Use hcom relay for cross-device coordination
- Bridge hcom TUI into our agent dashboard
- Explore replacing Valkey file-fallback with hcom SQLite

**Config Needed**:
```yaml
agent_communication:
  primary: valkey          # intra-session
  cross_terminal: hcom     # cross-terminal (optional)
  hcom:
    enabled: false
    auto_install: false
    collision_detection: true
    relay_enabled: false
```

**Tests to Write**:
1. test_hcom_installed(): verify hcom binary available
2. test_hcom_bridge_send(): send message via bridge, verify in hcom db
3. test_hcom_collision_detection(): simulate concurrent edits, verify notification
4. test_hcom_bridge_disabled(): verify no-op when hcom not installed
5. test_hcom_session_registration(): verify Cognitive OS session registers with hcom
6. Integration test: two Claude Code sessions communicating via hcom

**Estimated Total Effort**: 5-7 days for Phase 1+2, optional Phase 3 adds 2 days.

**Risks**:
- hcom is a young project (created 2025-07-21), API may change
- Rust binary means we can't patch/extend easily (unlike our Python libs)
- SQLite WAL contention possible if both hcom and Engram use SQLite heavily
- PTY wrapping (`hcom claude`) may interfere with our hook chain

**Recommendation**: Integrate as an OPTIONAL cross-terminal layer (Phase 1 only initially). Keep Valkey as the primary intra-session bus. hcom fills a gap we don't currently cover (multi-terminal coordination) without requiring us to rewrite our existing bus infrastructure.

**Where**: rules/agent-communication.md, lib/hcom_bridge.py (new), hooks/hcom-bridge.sh (new), cognitive-os.yaml
**Learned**: hcom uses SQLite+TCP for zero-dependency agent comms. It's complementary to our Valkey bus, not a replacement. The file collision detection is the most immediately useful feature for our session-concurrency system.

title: Research: Claude-Code-Usage-Monitor integration analysis
content:
**What**: Research analysis of github.com/Maciek-roboblog/Claude-Code-Usage-Monitor (v3.1.0, MIT, 7178 stars, Python 3.9+) for potential integration into Cognitive OS cost tracking.

**How It Works**:
- Reads JSONL files from ~/.claude/projects/ recursively (Claude Code's native session storage)
- Parses: timestamp, input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_tokens, cache_read_tokens, model, cost_usd, message_id, request_id
- P90 calculation uses Python statistics.quantiles() (90th percentile) -- descriptive stats, NOT ML
- Burn rate = total tokens / duration_minutes; cost_per_hour = (cost_usd / duration_min) * 60
- Token projection = current_tokens + (tokens_per_minute * remaining_minutes) -- linear extrapolation
- Supports plans: Pro (19k), Max5 (88k), Max20 (220k), Custom (P90-adaptive)
- Three-layer arch: UI (Rich terminal) -> Monitoring Orchestrator -> Foundation (data models, analysis)

**Complementarity with our cost tracking**:
- OUR system (lib/cost_dashboard.py + metrics/cost-events.jsonl): tracks agent-level costs per skill/task, model routing decisions, budget governance. Writes our own JSONL format.
- THEIR system: tracks Claude Code's raw token consumption from Claude's native JSONL files. Provides real-time terminal dashboard, burn rate, session limit predictions.
- KEY GAP: We don't read Claude Code's native usage files at all. We only track what we log ourselves. They read the ground truth.
- OVERLAP: Both calculate per-model costs with identical pricing tables.

**Integration Recommendation: COMPANION TOOL (not embed)**:
1. Standalone app -- no library API, tightly coupled to Rich terminal UI
2. Dependencies (numpy, rich, pydantic, sentry-sdk) would bloat our stack
3. Their JSONL reader (data/reader.py) is the most valuable extractable piece -- ~200 LOC that parses Claude's native session files
4. Recommend as companion tool for real-time monitoring; extract their JSONL reader pattern for our own ground-truth cost reconciliation

**Reusable Algorithms**:
- P90 calculator: Simple quantiles(), trivially reimplementable in 20 LOC
- Burn rate: Linear extrapolation, trivially reimplementable
- Pricing table: Identical to ours, no value in importing
- Session block parsing: Useful pattern for reading Claude's native data format

**What We Could Build Instead**:
1. A lightweight reader (lib/claude_usage_reader.py) that reads ~/.claude/projects/*.jsonl
2. A reconciliation function that compares our cost-events.jsonl against Claude's ground truth
3. Hook or session-end check that reports discrepancy between our tracked costs and actual Claude usage
4. Estimated effort: 2-3 days (small task, single service)

**Tests We'd Write**:
- Unit: parse Claude JSONL format (mock fixtures from their test suite patterns)
- Unit: cost reconciliation logic (our events vs Claude ground truth)
- Unit: burn rate calculation with known inputs
- Integration: end-to-end read from sample ~/.claude/projects/ directory
- Edge cases: empty files, malformed JSONL, missing fields, timezone handling

**Where**: lib/cost_dashboard.py, lib/cost_predictor.py, metrics/cost-events.jsonl (our existing), ~/.claude/projects/*.jsonl (Claude's native data)

**Learned**: 
- Claude Code stores session data as JSONL in ~/.claude/projects/ with fields: timestamp, input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_tokens, cache_read_tokens, model, cost_usd
- The "ML predictions" marketing is overstated -- it's percentile calculations and linear extrapolation, not machine learning
- 7178 stars indicates strong community interest in usage monitoring -- validates the need
- Cache token pricing: creation at 1.25x input rate, read at 0.1x input rate

title: Trail of Bits Skills Repository — Integration Analysis
content:
**What**: Full analysis of trailofbits/skills repo for integration into Cognitive OS security layer.

**Repository**: https://github.com/trailofbits/skills
**Stars**: 4,041 | **License**: CC-BY-SA-4.0 | **Last updated**: 2026-03-28
**Format**: Claude Code plugin marketplace (uses SKILL.md with YAML frontmatter)

## Inventory: 36 plugins, 62 SKILL.md files

### Security-Relevant Skills (HIGH PRIORITY for Cognitive OS)
| Plugin | Skills | Relevance to COS |
|--------|--------|-------------------|
| static-analysis | codeql, sarif-parsing, semgrep | Direct complement to security-scanning.md |
| semgrep-rule-creator | 1 | Enhances our Semgrep SAST integration |
| semgrep-rule-variant-creator | 1 | Port rules across languages |
| insecure-defaults | 1 | New capability: fail-open detection |
| supply-chain-risk-auditor | 1 | New capability: dependency risk assessment |
| variant-analysis | 1 | New capability: pattern-based vuln hunting |
| sharp-edges | 1 | New capability: dangerous API detection |
| fp-check | 1 | New capability: false positive verification |
| differential-review | 1 | Enhances code review with git history |
| agentic-actions-auditor | 1 | New: audit GitHub Actions for AI agent vulns |
| audit-context-building | 1 | Deep architectural context for audits |

### Smart Contract Security
| Plugin | Skills | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| building-secure-contracts | multiple | 6 blockchain scanners |
| entry-point-analyzer | 1 | State-changing entry points |
| spec-to-code-compliance | 1 | Blockchain spec compliance |

### Verification/Testing
| Plugin | Skills | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| constant-time-analysis | 1 | Timing side-channel detection (has Python pkg + tests) |
| property-based-testing | 1 | Multi-language property testing |
| zeroize-audit | 1 | Secret zeroization in C/C++/Rust |
| testing-handbook-skills | multiple | Fuzzers, sanitizers, coverage |

### Development/Utility (lower priority)
| Plugin | Skills | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| ask-questions-if-underspecified | 1 | We have clarification-gate |
| modern-python | 1 | uv/ruff/pytest guidance |
| git-cleanup | 1 | Worktree cleanup |
| skill-improver | 1 | Iterative skill refinement |
| workflow-skill-design | 1 | Design patterns for skills |

## Skill Format Compatibility

FULLY COMPATIBLE with Claude Code / Cognitive OS:
- Uses `SKILL.md` with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `allowed-tools`)
- Structure: `skills/<name>/SKILL.md` + optional `references/`, `workflows/`, `scripts/`
- Plugin wrapper: `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` (name, version, description, author)
- Can be installed directly via `/plugin marketplace add trailofbits/skills`
- Can also be manually copied: `cp -r plugins/<name>/skills/<skill>/ .claude/skills/`

## License: CC-BY-SA-4.0 Implications

**What CC-BY-SA-4.0 requires**:
1. **Attribution (BY)**: Must credit Trail of Bits and link to original
2. **ShareAlike (SA)**: Derivative works must use same or compatible license
3. **NOT a software license**: Designed for creative content, not code

**Can we bundle them?** YES, with conditions:
- Must include attribution (author name, link to original repo)
- If we MODIFY skill content, our modifications must also be CC-BY-SA-4.0
- If we create NEW skills inspired by (but not derived from) theirs: no restriction
- Our own skills under different licenses are fine as long as not derivatives

**Practical approach**:
- Install as git submodule or separate directory, NOT mixed with our MIT/proprietary skills
- Add ATTRIBUTION.md referencing Trail of Bits
- Keep modified copies clearly marked with original attribution
- Our wrapper/integration code (hooks, routing) is our own work, not derivative

**CAUTION**: CC-BY-SA-4.0 is NOT on our license-policy.md approved list. It's not AGPL/SSPL (not blocked), but it IS copyleft for content. The ShareAlike clause means any modifications to their skill text must be shared under same terms. This is acceptable for skills (they're instructional text, not compiled code), but should be documented.

## Tests

Minimal automated testing:
- CI validates SKILL.md frontmatter (name, description present)
- CI checks for hardcoded paths and personal emails
- CI validates plugin.json metadata
- Only `constant-time-analysis` has actual Python unit tests
- No skill behavior/output tests exist

## Integration Plan

### Phase 1: Direct Installation (1-2 hours)
1. Install via marketplace: `/plugin marketplace add trailofbits/skills`
2. Or clone as submodule: `git submodule add https://github.com/trailofbits/skills.git .claude/plugins/trailofbits-skills`
3. Add attribution file

### Phase 2: Skill Routing Integration (2-4 hours)
Update `rules/skill-management.md` routing table:
| Task Type | Primary Skill | Fallback |
|---|---|---|
| Security scan | `/semgrep-scan` | `trailofbits/static-analysis` |
| Vuln hunting | `trailofbits/variant-analysis` | `trailofbits/audit-context-building` |
| Dependency audit | `trailofbits/supply-chain-risk-auditor` | -- |
| Insecure config | `trailofbits/insecure-defaults` | -- |
| Semgrep rules | `trailofbits/semgrep-rule-creator` | -- |
| Code review | `/self-review` | `trailofbits/differential-review` |

### Phase 3: Quality Integration (4-8 hours)
- Write integration tests: verify skills load, trigger correctly, produce structured output
- Add to pentesting-readiness.md: new test cases for supply chain + insecure defaults
- Add to security-scanning.md: reference ToB skills as complementary tools
- Create `customizations/` entries for ToB skills with appropriate tool restrictions

### Phase 4: Adaptation (optional, 4-8 hours)
- Fork high-value skills that need modification (under CC-BY-SA-4.0)
- Add Cognitive OS-specific frontmatter (version, auto-generated, tech)
- Wire into consequence system for performance tracking

## Tests We'd Write
1. Skill loading test: each ToB skill loads with valid frontmatter
2. Tool restriction test: skills only request allowed tools
3. Trigger accuracy test: security keywords trigger correct ToB skill
4. Output format test: skills produce structured findings (not just prose)
5. Integration test: ToB skills work alongside our security-scanning hook
6. Attribution test: verify ATTRIBUTION.md exists and references ToB

## Estimated Effort
- Phase 1 (install): 1-2 hours
- Phase 2 (routing): 2-4 hours
- Phase 3 (quality): 4-8 hours
- Phase 4 (adapt): 4-8 hours (optional)
- Total: 7-22 hours depending on depth

**Where**: rules/skill-management.md, rules/security-scanning.md, rules/pentesting-readiness.md, .claude/plugins/
**Learned**: CC-BY-SA-4.0 is copyleft for content — keep ToB skills in separate directory, never mix with our proprietary skills. The plugin format is 100% compatible with Claude Code SKILL.md convention. ToB skills are instruction-heavy (no code to compile), making them ideal for direct use.

title: Decision: Cognitive OS goes multi-tool — not Claude Code-only
content:
**What**: Cognitive OS will support multiple AI coding tools, not just Claude Code. Target: OpenCode, Aider, Cursor, Codex, Gemini CLI.

**Why**: The ecosystem is fragmenting. Users shouldn't be locked to Claude Code. Our Python libs are already tool-agnostic. MCP is a universal bridge. Being multi-tool is a massive differentiator — no other agent OS does this.

**Strategy — 3 layers of portability**:

1. **Adapter layer**: Translate hooks between tool formats. Claude Code hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse) → OpenCode equivalents → Aider equivalents. One adapter per tool.

2. **MCP as universal bridge**: MCP is supported by Claude Code, Cursor, Continue, Cline, and growing. Our MCP servers (Engram, Context7) already work everywhere. Expand: expose smart_infra, cost_dashboard, model_router as MCP tools.

3. **Rules portability**: Auto-transform our .claude/rules/*.md → .cursorrules, .aider, OpenCode formats. The CONTENT is the same — only the file format/location changes.

**What's already portable (no work needed)**:
- All Python libs (lib/*.py) — tool-agnostic
- MCP servers (Engram, Context7)
- Docker infrastructure (smart_infra, docker-compose)
- Rules content (markdown)

**What needs adapters**:
- Hooks (Claude Code specific format)
- Skills (SKILL.md is Claude Code format)
- settings.json (Claude Code specific)

**Priority**: Phase 2 roadmap item. Start with OpenCode (open-source, growing fast) as second supported tool.

**Where**: New section in docs/roadmap.md, new directory: adapters/

**Learned**: hcom already supports Claude, Gemini, Codex, OpenCode — proves multi-tool is viable. recall also supports OpenCode/Codex/Factory.

title: Multi-tool architecture and ecosystem integrations documented
content:
**What**: Added multi-tool adapter architecture and 7 ecosystem integration plans to roadmap and architecture docs.
**Why**: Cognitive OS is evolving from Claude Code-only to a universal agent OS. The adapter layer enables support for OpenCode, Aider, Cursor, and Codex CLI.
**Where**: docs/roadmap.md (new "Multi-Tool Support" cross-cutting section + "Ecosystem Integrations (ADOPT)" in Phase 1), docs/architecture.md (new "Multi-Tool Adapter Architecture" section), CHANGELOG.md (2 new entries under [Unreleased]).
**Learned**: 3 layers are already portable (Python libs, MCP servers, Docker infra). Only hooks and rules need adapters. 7 ecosystem tools approved for integration: agnix (linter), claude-code-action (GitHub Actions), parry (prompt injection scanner), Trail of Bits Skills (62 security skills), recall (conversation search), Usage Monitor (cost reconciliation), hcom (cross-terminal communication).

title: Decision: Plugin marketplace architecture — cos install as npm moment
content:
**What**: Build a plugin marketplace for Cognitive OS — `cos install @luum/safety-mesh`, `cos publish`, `cos search`. The "npm moment" for AI agent tools.

**Why**: 114+ tools in awesome-claude-code alone. Hundreds more across the ecosystem. Manual integration is unsustainable. Need a package manager with security auditing built-in.

**Key decisions**:

1. **Two modes of package creation**:
   - **Generated**: COS reads a repo's stack/structure and AUTO-GENERATES skills, hooks, rules adapted for that tool. The OS has the intelligence (skill-creator, auto-skill-generation) to do this.
   - **Curated**: Pre-built packages from trusted sources (like Trail of Bits skills). Downloaded as-is with attribution.

2. **Security-first**: Every package goes through an audit pipeline BEFORE installation:
   - License check (block AGPL/SSPL/GPL per our policy)
   - Secret scanning (no hardcoded credentials)
   - Prompt injection scanning (parry on all skill content)
   - Dependency analysis (no malicious deps)
   - Sandbox execution (test in worktree before merging)
   - Signature verification (trusted publishers)

3. **Registry architecture**:
   - Public registry: GitHub-based (repos with `cos-package.yaml` manifest)
   - Private registry: Local/enterprise (file-based or S3)
   - Discovery: `cos search security` → finds packages across registries

4. **Package format**: `cos-package.yaml` manifest:
   ```yaml
   name: "@luum/safety-mesh"
   version: "1.0.0"
   license: "Apache-2.0"
   type: skill|hook|rule|plugin|bundle
   requires: [parry, engram]
   files: [skills/, hooks/, rules/]
   audit:
     license_verified: true
     secret_scan: passed
     injection_scan: passed
   ```

**Where**: `cmd/cos/` (Go CLI already exists), `lib/package_manager.py`, `rules/package-security.md`

**Learned**: The ecosystem is fragmented — awesome-claude-code has 114 repos, most are point tools. A marketplace that aggregates, audits, and standardizes them is a massive differentiator. No one else is doing this.

title: Claude Code Action GitHub workflows added
content:
**What**: Created 3 GitHub Actions workflows for claude-code-action integration plus integration tests.
**Why**: Enable automated Claude interactions on PRs/issues: interactive mentions, automated PR review for critical paths, and issue triage.
**Where**: 
- `.github/workflows/claude-interactive.yml` -- @claude mentions on PRs and issues
- `.github/workflows/claude-pr-review.yml` -- auto-review on changes to hooks/rules/skills/lib/cognitive-os.yaml
- `.github/workflows/claude-issue-triage.yml` -- auto-triage new issues with complexity/labels/effort
- `tests/integration/test_ecosystem_tools.py` -- 4 tests validating workflow presence and YAML validity
**Learned**: PyYAML parses the bare `on` key as boolean `True`, not string `"on"`. Tests must check for both `"on" in parsed or True in parsed` when validating GitHub Actions YAML files.

title: Ecosystem tools integration: Parry, Recall, Hcom
content:
**What**: Integrated 3 ecosystem tools into Cognitive OS as optional addons: Parry (ML prompt injection scanner), Recall (conversation transcript search), and Hcom (cross-terminal agent communication).
**Why**: Extend security, memory search, and multi-session coordination capabilities without hard dependencies.
**Where**: 
- rules/parry-integration.md -- Parry prompt injection scanner rule
- skills/recall-search/SKILL.md -- Recall conversation search skill
- rules/hcom-integration.md -- Hcom cross-terminal communication rule
- cognitive-os.yaml -- Added security.parry and agent_communication.hcom config sections
- tests/integration/test_ecosystem_tools.py -- 9 new tests (config presence, rule/skill existence, binary availability)
**Learned**: All three tools follow graceful degradation pattern -- they are optional layers that enhance existing capabilities (content-policy.sh, Engram mem_search, Valkey pub/sub) without replacing them. Tests skip cleanly when binaries are not installed.

title: Trail of Bits skills + Usage Monitor integration
content:
**What**: Integrated Trail of Bits security skills (62 professional audit skills) as optional git submodule, and created a Claude Usage Reader library for cost reconciliation against ground-truth Claude Code session data.

**Why**: Trail of Bits skills complement existing Semgrep SAST with professional security audit patterns (code auditing, supply chain, smart contracts, prompt security). The usage reader enables cost reconciliation by reading Claude Code's native JSONL session files (~/.claude/projects/*.jsonl).

**Where**:
- `ATTRIBUTION.md` -- CC-BY-SA-4.0 license attribution for Trail of Bits skills
- `scripts/install-tob-skills.sh` -- git submodule installer for `.claude/plugins/trailofbits-skills/`
- `rules/trailofbits-skills.md` -- documentation and integration notes
- `lib/claude_usage_reader.py` -- reads Claude Code session JSONL, calculates costs, reconciles against cost-events.jsonl
- `tests/unit/test_claude_usage_reader.py` -- 11 unit tests
- `tests/integration/test_ecosystem_tools.py` -- 6 integration tests added (TestTrailOfBitsIntegration + TestUsageMonitorIntegration)

**Learned**: Trail of Bits skills are CC-BY-SA-4.0 licensed -- derivatives must also use that license. The usage reader gracefully handles missing/malformed files and unknown models. All 17 new tests pass.

title: Plugin Marketplace design documented in roadmap
content:
**What**: Documented the Plugin Marketplace as the next major feature in docs/roadmap.md (Phase 4) and CHANGELOG.md. Replaced the brief placeholder with a full design covering two modes (Generated vs Curated), a mandatory 6-gate security audit pipeline (license, secret scan, injection scan, dependency audit, sandbox test, signature verify), cos-package.yaml manifest format, CLI commands (install/publish/search/audit/list/update/remove), registry architecture (public/curated/private), and existing infrastructure to leverage.

**Why**: The plugin marketplace is the "npm moment" for AI agent tools -- it enables sharing skills, hooks, rules, and bundles across projects and teams with security guarantees. This is the next major feature after ecosystem integrations.

**Where**: docs/roadmap.md (Phase 4 section rewritten, Phase 5 agent package manager updated, Contributing section updated), CHANGELOG.md (added under Unreleased)

**Learned**: The existing infrastructure already covers 4 of 6 security gates (license_guard.py, secret-detector.sh, parry-guard, git worktrees). Only dependency audit and signature verification need to be built new. The Go CLI in cmd/cos/ already has v0.1 scaffolding. Implementation requires full SDD pipeline.

title: Agnix linter integration into Cognitive OS
content:
**What**: Integrated the agnix agent configuration linter as an ecosystem tool in Cognitive OS.

**Why**: Agnix provides automated linting of agent config files (SKILL.md, rules, agent definitions) to catch best-practice violations before they reach production.

**Where**:
- `.agnix.toml` -- configuration file at project root (target: claude-code, severity: warning)
- `hooks/agnix-lint.sh` -- PostToolUse hook on Edit|Write, triggers on .claude/, rules/, skills/, agents/ paths
- `tests/unit/test_agnix_integration.py` -- 19 unit tests covering config validity, hook gating, phase-aware behavior
- `tests/integration/test_ecosystem_tools.py` -- TestAgnixIntegration class added with 6 integration tests
- `rules/ecosystem-tools.md` -- new rule documenting all integrated external tools

**Learned**:
- Hook follows the graceful degradation pattern (exit 0 if agnix not installed)
- Phase-aware: advisory (exit 0) in reconstruction/stabilization, blocking (exit 2) for errors in production/maintenance
- Uses common.sh functions: require_tool, get_phase, check_capability_level, check_private_mode, read_stdin_json, stdin_field
- Uses safe-jsonl.sh for metrics logging to agnix-findings.jsonl
- Path matching uses bash case statement for .claude/*, rules/*, skills/*, agents/* patterns
- All 25 tests pass (19 unit + 6 integration, 2 integration skipped when agnix not installed)

title: WATCH repos competitive analysis - 6 repos deep dive (March 2026)
content:
**What**: Deep analysis of 6 WATCH repos for patterns/ideas to adopt in Cognitive OS.

## 1. cc-sessions (GWUDCAP, 1.6K stars, MIT, JS)
**What it does**: Opinionated Claude Code workflow framework enforcing DAIC (Discussion-Alignment-Implementation-Check) gates. Blocks Write/Edit tools until todos are approved, creates git branches per task, persists task state across sessions.

**Key patterns worth extracting**:
- DAIC gating: Write/Edit tools blocked until explicit approval via trigger phrases. Scope creep detection reverts to discussion mode if Claude attempts unapproved changes.
- Todo validation: Approved todos are "locked" — tracked against actual implementation. System detects when agent works outside approved scope.
- 5 specialized sub-agents (context-gathering, logging, code-review, context-refinement, service-documentation) each getting full conversation transcript.
- Structured output markers ([PROPOSAL], [STATUS], [PLAN]) signal when user input needed.
- Task persistence via markdown+YAML frontmatter with git branch association.

**What we'd take**:
- The todo-locking + scope-creep-detection pattern. We have acceptance criteria and blast radius, but no runtime enforcement that detects mid-execution scope drift. Could enhance our PostToolUse hooks.
- Trigger phrase concept for workflow state transitions (natural language gates).
- The task-branch binding pattern (task files reference git branches, edits restricted to task branch).

**What we'd skip**:
- DAIC gating would conflict with our adaptive-bypass for trivial tasks.
- Their sub-agent architecture is simpler than ours (no model routing, no trust scores).
- Kickstart onboarding tutorial — nice but not a priority.

**Integration effort**: LOW-MEDIUM. Scope-creep detection could be a new PostToolUse hook (~200 lines). Task-branch binding is a git hook (~100 lines).

---

## 2. Claude Code System Prompts (Piebald-AI, 6.9K stars, MIT, JS)
**What it does**: Documents Claude Code's complete system prompt architecture across 135+ versions. Extracts prompts from compiled source, including tool descriptions, sub-agent prompts, security monitors, and utility functions.

**Key patterns worth extracting**:
- Verification Specialist sub-agent (~2,453 tokens) issues PASS/FAIL verdicts — similar to our sdd-verify but built into Claude Code natively.
- Two-part security monitor (~5,734 tokens) evaluates autonomous actions against block/allow rules.
- Dream memory agent (~727 tokens) for session memory consolidation.
- Plan Agent (~636 tokens) with multi-phase reasoning.
- Tool preferences encoded: "Prefer Edit tool instead of sed/awk" — similar to our result-management rules.

**What we'd take**:
- Study the security monitor architecture for our agent-security rules. Their block/allow pattern is more granular than our permission levels.
- Dream memory agent concept — we do session summaries but not "memory consolidation" that synthesizes across sessions.
- Track version changes to understand where Claude Code is heading (features we might duplicate or conflict with).

**What we'd skip**:
- Raw prompt text (copyright concerns, and it changes every release).
- Their tool descriptions (we already have our own).

**Integration effort**: LOW. Primarily reference material. Memory consolidation concept could become a new skill (~300 lines).

---

## 3. tweakcc (Piebald-AI, 1.5K stars, MIT, TypeScript)
**What it does**: Patches Claude Code's cli.js to inject custom system prompts, custom toolsets, themes, AGENTS.md support, and hidden features. Works on both npm and native installs.

**Key patterns worth extracting**:
- Custom toolset creation via /toolset command — extends Claude Code's native commands.
- AGENTS.md support — alternative to CLAUDE.md with agent-specific configs.
- Subagent model configuration — lets you pick which model each subagent uses (Plan, Explore, general).
- Session memory unlock.
- Sandboxed script execution via Node.js --experimental-permission flag.

**What we'd take**:
- AGENTS.md pattern: if Claude Code natively supports AGENTS.md, we should investigate making our rules/skills compatible.
- Subagent model config pattern — we already have model-routing but tweakcc shows Claude Code may have native support for per-subagent model selection.
- Awareness of what can be patched vs what's stable API — helps us avoid building on unstable internals.

**What we'd skip**:
- The patching mechanism itself (fragile, breaks on updates).
- Themes, spinners, UI customization (not relevant to our runtime).
- Token counter (we have our own cost tracking).

**Integration effort**: NONE directly. This is intelligence about Claude Code internals, not code to adopt.

---

## 4. SPARC (ruvnet, 442 stars, Apache-2.0, Python)
**What it does**: Spec-driven development framework with 5 phases: Specification, Pseudocode, Architecture, Refinement, Completion. Includes CLI (v0.87.7) with multi-LLM support.

**Key patterns worth extracting**:
- Their 5-phase pipeline vs our SDD: S(spec)=our propose+spec, P(pseudocode)=our design, A(architecture)=our design, R(refinement)=our verify+improve, C(completion)=our archive. Very similar.
- Pseudocode phase as explicit intermediate step between spec and implementation — we go directly from design to tasks.
- Multi-LLM provider support (Anthropic, OpenAI, OpenRouter) — we have this in model-routing but they have a CLI-native approach.
- Human-in-the-loop controls integrated into the pipeline.

**What we'd take**:
- The pseudocode/algorithm design step. Our design phase covers architecture but not pseudocode. Adding a pseudocode artifact between design and tasks could improve implementation quality.
- Their "cowboy mode" concept (skip safety gates) — similar to our adaptive-bypass but more explicit.

**What we'd skip**:
- "Consciousness integration" and "quantum state calculations" — marketing fluff.
- "Symbolic reasoning" — vague claims without substance.
- Their CLI — we have our own orchestration.

**Integration effort**: LOW. Adding a pseudocode step to SDD is a documentation/process change, not a code change.

---

## 5. AgentSys (avifenesh, 662 stars, MIT, JS)
**What it does**: Modular agent orchestration platform with 19 plugins, 47 agents, 40 skills. Marketplace model where each plugin is a standalone repo. Supports 5+ AI code environments (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Kiro, OpenCode).

**Key patterns worth extracting**:
- Plugin architecture: Command plugins (contribute slash commands), Skill-only plugins (reusable units), Cross-plugin agents (invoke skills from multiple plugins).
- Multi-platform adapters: Same skills work across Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, etc.
- agnix linter: 342 rules (102 auto-fixable) for validating agent configurations across platforms.
- Certainty grading: Findings carry HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW certainty levels.
- Phase gates in pipelines: discovery -> planning -> implementation -> review -> ship.
- Cost benchmark: "Sonnet + agentsys produced more output than raw Opus at 40% lower cost."

**What we'd take**:
- Multi-platform adapter pattern. If we ever want Cognitive OS to work outside Claude Code, their adapter architecture (adapters/codex, adapters/opencode) is the template.
- agnix-style configuration linter. We have cognitive-os-test but a dedicated config validator with auto-fix would be valuable.
- Certainty grading on findings — complements our trust score system.
- Their plugin marketplace model — each skill as a standalone installable repo.

**What we'd skip**:
- AGENTS.md format (we have CLAUDE.md + SKILL.md).
- Their specific agents/skills (different focus).
- Multi-platform support right now (we're Claude Code-only for now).

**Integration effort**: MEDIUM. Config linter is ~500 lines. Multi-platform adapters would be a major refactor (only if needed).

---

## 6. cc-devops-skills (akin-ozer, 141 stars, Apache-2.0, Python)
**What it does**: 31 DevOps skill pack with generator+validator pairs for Terraform, K8s, Helm, CI/CD, Docker, observability. Graceful degradation when tools unavailable.

**Skills inventory** (31 total):
- IaC: terraform, terragrunt, ansible (generator+validator each)
- CI/CD: github-actions, gitlab-ci, azure-pipelines, jenkinsfile (generator+validator)
- Containers/K8s: dockerfile, k8s-yaml, helm (generator+validator), k8s-debug
- Observability: promql, logql, fluentbit, loki-config (generators+validators)
- Supporting: bash-script, makefile (generator+validator)

**Key patterns worth extracting**:
- Generator+Validator pair pattern: Every generator has a matching validator. terraform-generator creates, terraform-validator runs "terraform fmt -> tflint -> terraform validate -> Checkov -> optional plan."
- Multi-stage validation pipelines: Each validator chains multiple tools (e.g., helm: lint -> template -> kubeconform -> cluster dry-run).
- Graceful degradation: Validators report which checks were skipped when tools unavailable.
- .claude-plugin + .codex-plugin dual distribution.

**What we'd take**:
- Cherry-pick specific skills: k8s-debug, terraform-validator, github-actions-validator would be immediately useful.
- Generator+Validator pair pattern as a template for our own skill creation.
- Multi-stage validation pipeline pattern — similar to our staged-verification but specialized for infra.
- Graceful degradation pattern for validators.

**What we'd skip**:
- Skills we don't need (Jenkins, Azure Pipelines, Ansible — project-dependent).
- Their plugin format (we have our own SKILL.md format).

**Integration effort**: LOW per skill. Each skill is self-contained. Converting to SKILL.md format is straightforward (~1 hour per skill).

---

## Priority Adoption Ranking

1. **Scope-creep detection** (from cc-sessions) — HIGH VALUE, fills a gap in our PostToolUse chain
2. **Config linter with auto-fix** (from AgentSys) — HIGH VALUE, improves self-testing
3. **Generator+Validator pair pattern** (from cc-devops-skills) — MEDIUM VALUE, skill creation template
4. **Dream memory consolidation** (from system prompts) — MEDIUM VALUE, cross-session synthesis
5. **Multi-platform adapter architecture** (from AgentSys) — LOW PRIORITY NOW, high value later
6. **Pseudocode phase in SDD** (from SPARC) — LOW PRIORITY, incremental improvement
7. **Certainty grading** (from AgentSys) — already partially covered by trust scores

title: WATCH Repo Research: 6 repos analyzed for Cognitive OS patterns
content:
**What**: Deep analysis of 6 WATCH repos for patterns extractable to Cognitive OS.

## 1. claude-esp (phiat/claude-esp) — 83 stars, Go, MIT
**What it does**: Go TUI that streams Claude Code's hidden output (thinking, tool calls, subagents) to a separate terminal in real-time. Uses fsnotify to watch JSONL session files at ~/.claude/projects/.
**Key patterns**: (a) JSONL session file parsing from ~/.claude/projects/{path}/{session}.jsonl, (b) subagent discovery via subagents/agent-{id}.jsonl + .meta.json, (c) 50ms debounce on file writes, (d) hierarchical tree view of sessions+subagents, (e) token usage tracking per session.
**What we'd take**: The JSONL parsing logic for session observability. Could feed our agent-kpis and performance-monitoring with real Claude Code session data instead of estimating. The subagent discovery pattern is directly useful for our orchestrator-mode executor monitoring.
**What we'd skip**: The TUI itself (we have different UI needs). The Go implementation (our stack is Python/shell).
**Integration effort**: LOW. Port the JSONL parsing patterns to Python. Add a session-watcher module to lib/ that reads Claude session files for real metrics.

## 2. Crystal/Nimbalyst (stravu/crystal) — 2997 stars, TypeScript, MIT
**What it does**: Desktop Electron app for running multiple Claude Code sessions in parallel git worktrees. Now deprecated, replaced by Nimbalyst (closed-source).
**Key patterns**: (a) Git worktree-based session isolation (exactly what we do), (b) side-by-side comparison of different approaches, (c) Playwright-based testing of the app itself.
**What we'd take**: Validation that our worktree-based session isolation (session-concurrency.md) is the right approach — this was a 3K-star product built on the same concept. Their UX for comparing approaches side-by-side is worth noting for future dashboard work.
**What we'd skip**: Everything — it's deprecated and Electron-based. No extractable code.
**Integration effort**: NONE. Conceptual validation only.

## 3. RIPER Workflow (tony/claude-code-riper-5) — 73 stars, MIT
**What it does**: Claude Code custom commands implementing Research/Innovate/Plan/Execute/Review phases. Each phase has distinct read/write permissions. Uses a memory-bank with branch-aware persistence.
**Key patterns**: (a) 3-agent consolidated architecture: research-innovate, plan-execute, review, (b) branch-aware memory bank (.claude/memory-bank/{branch}/plans|reviews|sessions), (c) strict mode enforcement per phase (research=read-only, execute=full access), (d) separate commands per phase (/riper/research, /riper/plan, etc.), (e) memory auto-backup.
**What we'd take**: Branch-aware memory is interesting — our Engram is project-scoped but not branch-scoped. For feature branches, having branch-aware context could reduce noise. The 3-agent consolidation (vs our many phases) is worth considering for simpler workflows. Their "Innovate" phase (brainstorming without implementation pressure) maps to our sdd-explore but is more explicitly creativity-focused.
**What we'd skip**: The overall framework is much simpler than ours and lacks quality gates, cost governance, trust scoring. Not a replacement, just a simpler alternative for small tasks.
**Integration effort**: LOW. Add optional branch-scoping to Engram topic keys. Consider a simplified "RIPER-lite" workflow for small/medium tasks that bypasses full SDD.

## 4. Vibe-Log (vibe-log/vibe-log-cli) — 310 stars, TypeScript, MIT
**What it does**: CLI tool that analyzes Claude Code sessions locally, generates productivity reports and standup summaries. Includes prompt quality scoring with coach personalities.
**Key patterns**: (a) Parallel sub-agent processing of session data, (b) privacy-first sanitization (removes code, credentials, paths while preserving context), (c) prompt quality scoring (0-100) with actionable feedback, (d) HTML report generation, (e) "coach personalities" for different feedback styles, (f) standup generation from session history.
**What we'd take**: Prompt quality scoring concept — we have clarification-gate (ambiguity scoring) but not quality scoring of user prompts. Their sanitization approach for sharing session data is useful for export/reporting. The standup generation from sessions is a natural fit for our mem_session_summary.
**What we'd skip**: The HTML reports (we use Engram). The coach personalities (gimmicky). Cloud sync features.
**Integration effort**: LOW-MEDIUM. Add a prompt-quality-score to our clarification-gate or as a separate advisory hook. Add sanitization to session summary exports.

## 5. MyCoder (drivecore/mycoder) — 565 stars, TypeScript, MIT
**What it does**: CLI-based AI coding agent with multi-agent architecture, supporting Anthropic/OpenAI/Ollama. Features sub-agent spawning, message compaction, GitHub issue/PR integration, and interactive correction (Ctrl+M).
**Key patterns**: (a) Interactive correction during execution (Ctrl+M to inject guidance mid-agent), (b) message compaction for context window management, (c) cascading config (.js/.yaml/.toml/.json at project/user level), (d) GitHub comment trigger (/mycoder in issues), (e) self-modifying capability (built by writing itself), (f) system browser reuse vs Playwright.
**What we'd take**: Interactive mid-execution correction is directly relevant to our split-and-resume pattern. Their approach is simpler — just inject a message rather than our NEEDS_CLARIFICATION protocol. GitHub comment triggers for automation align with our singularity/webhook-trigger pattern.
**What we'd skip**: Their overall architecture (we're more comprehensive). Multi-provider support (we have model-routing). The CLI itself.
**Integration effort**: LOW. The mid-execution correction pattern could simplify our split-and-resume. GitHub comment trigger is a good addition for singularity.

## 6. Continue (continuedev/continue) — 32.1K stars, TypeScript, Apache-2.0
**What it does**: Open-source platform for source-controlled AI checks in CI. Checks are markdown files in .continue/checks/ with YAML frontmatter, acting as GitHub status checks on PRs.
**Key patterns**: (a) Checks as markdown files with YAML frontmatter (name, description) + natural language instructions, (b) .continue/rules/ for coding standards (24 rule files covering CSS, TypeScript, testing, docs, etc.), (c) Checks produce green/red status with suggested diffs, (d) CLI tool (cn) for local execution, (e) Rules are granular and specific (no-any-types.md, css-units.md, pure-function-unit-tests.md).
**What we'd take**: The checks-as-markdown-in-CI pattern is powerful. We have PostToolUse hooks but no CI-level AI checks. Their .continue/checks/ format (frontmatter + natural language) is simpler than our hook shell scripts. Their granular rule files (24 specific rules like "no-any-types") validate our approach of many specific rules vs few broad ones. The "suggested diffs" output format for checks is worth adopting for our adversarial-review.
**What we'd skip**: The VS Code/JetBrains IDE integration (not our scope). The cloud platform aspects.
**Integration effort**: MEDIUM. Create a CI check runner that executes our rules as GitHub status checks. Adopt their frontmatter format for a simplified check definition. Add "suggested diff" output to sdd-verify.

## Cross-Cutting Insights
1. **Session file parsing is a goldmine**: Both claude-esp and vibe-log parse ~/.claude/projects/ JSONL files. We should too for real metrics.
2. **Branch-aware memory**: RIPER does this, we don't. Worth adding to Engram.
3. **CI-level AI checks**: Continue's 32K stars prove this is high-value. We should add this capability.
4. **Simplicity has value**: RIPER's 5 phases vs our 8+ SDD phases. Consider a lite mode.
5. **Interactive correction**: MyCoder's Ctrl+M is simpler than our NEEDS_CLARIFICATION protocol.

## Priority Actions
1. HIGH: Parse Claude Code JSONL session files for real agent metrics (from claude-esp)
2. HIGH: CI check runner using markdown+frontmatter format (from Continue)
3. MEDIUM: Branch-aware Engram topic keys (from RIPER)
4. MEDIUM: Prompt quality scoring hook (from Vibe-Log)
5. LOW: Simplified RIPER-lite workflow mode for small tasks
6. LOW: Mid-execution message injection (from MyCoder)

title: Competitive landscape research: 7 WATCH repos deep analysis
content:
**What**: Deep analysis of 7 competitor/adjacent repos for patterns to adopt in Cognitive OS
**Why**: Strategic research to identify gaps, steal good ideas, and validate our approach
**Where**: N/A (research output, no files changed)
**Learned**: See full report below

---

# WATCH Repos Deep Analysis (2026-03-28)

## 1. Compound Engineering (EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin)
- Stars: 11.3K | MIT | TypeScript | Updated: 2026-03-28

### What it does
An AI development plugin implementing the "compound engineering" philosophy: each unit of engineering work should make subsequent units easier, not harder. Inverts the technical debt trajectory by emphasizing 80% planning/review, 20% execution. Provides 6 cyclical commands: ideate, brainstorm, plan, work, review, compound.

### Key patterns worth extracting
1. **Compound loop** (`/ce:compound`): Explicit "document what you learned" phase after every work cycle. We have Engram saves but no explicit "compound" step that forces retrospection.
2. **Progressive ceremony reduction**: Brainstorm commands short-circuit when ceremony is not needed. Similar to our adaptive-bypass but applied at the ideation level.
3. **Ideate phase**: Proactive improvement surfacing via divergent ideation + adversarial filtering. We lack a dedicated "find things to improve" command.
4. **Cross-provider format conversion**: Single source of truth synced to 9 AI tool formats.

### What we'd take
- **Compound phase concept**: Add a `/sdd-compound` or `/retrospect` skill that runs after archive, explicitly extracting and persisting patterns. Our archive phase is close but focused on docs, not learning extraction.
- **Ideate command**: A `/ce:ideate` equivalent that scans codebase for improvement opportunities (tech debt, patterns, refactoring candidates).

### What we'd skip
- Cross-provider sync (not our problem space)
- Their specific brainstorm Q&A flow (our SDD explore covers this)

### Integration effort
LOW. Adding a compound/retrospect skill is 1-2 days. Ideate command is medium (needs codebase scanning logic).

---

## 2. SuperClaude (SuperClaude-Org/SuperClaude_Framework)
- Stars: 22K | MIT | Python | Updated: 2026-03-28

### What it does
A meta-programming configuration framework with 30 slash commands, 20 specialized agents, and 7 behavioral modes. Installs as a Python package (pipx) and deploys commands/agents to ~/.claude/. Key innovation: cognitive personas (agents with domain expertise) and behavioral modes that change how Claude operates.

### Key patterns worth extracting
1. **Behavioral Modes**: 7 modes (Brainstorming, Business Panel, Deep Research, Introspection, Orchestration, Task Management, Token Efficiency) that shift Claude's behavior wholesale. We have phases but not "modes" that change cognitive style.
2. **ConfidenceChecker pattern**: Pre-execution confidence assessment with thresholds (>=90% proceed, 70-89% alternatives, <70% ask questions). More granular than our confidence-gate.
3. **Wave-Checkpoint-Wave parallel execution**: [Read files in parallel] -> Analyze -> [Edit files in parallel]. Claims 3.5x speedup.
4. **ReflexionPattern**: Cross-session error learning and prevention -- similar to our error-learning but with explicit "reflexion" pattern matching.
5. **PM Agent as central pattern**: Project management agent that enforces confidence checks, self-checks, and reflexion. Dedicated quality agent.

### What we'd take
- **Behavioral modes concept**: Token Efficiency mode and Introspection mode are interesting. We could add a `/mode` command that adjusts verbosity, model routing, and governance strictness.
- **Wave-Checkpoint-Wave**: Explicit parallel execution pattern for sub-agents. Our orchestrator could batch parallel reads, then sequential writes.
- **Pre-execution confidence scoring**: Extend our confidence-gate to do pre-execution assessment (not just post-execution gating).

### What we'd skip
- 20 specialized agents (our skill-based approach is more flexible)
- Their command naming convention (/sc:*) -- we already have our own
- PyPI distribution model (we use self-install.sh)

### Integration effort
